Immunomodulatory Drug Therapy: Lenalidomide, pomalidomide, and thalidomide exert anti-myeloma effects through cereblon-mediated degradation of IKZF1/IKZF3 transcription factors, direct anti-proliferative effects, immune modulation, and anti-angiogenic activity.

Anti-CD38 Monoclonal Antibody Therapy: Daratumumab and isatuximab target CD38, which is highly expressed on myeloma cells. These antibodies exert anti-myeloma effects through multiple mechanisms including ADCC, CDC, ADCP, and direct apoptosis induction.

Autologous Stem Cell Transplantation: High-dose melphalan followed by autologous stem cell transplant remains standard of care for transplant-eligible patients. It deepens treatment response and improves progression-free survival.

CAR-T Cell Therapy: BCMA-directed CAR-T cell therapies (idecabtagene vicleucel, ciltacabtagene autoleucel) are approved for relapsed/refractory myeloma. They target B cell maturation antigen expressed on myeloma cells.

Bispecific Antibody Therapy: Bispecific antibodies (teclistamab, elranatamab, talquetamab) redirect T cells to myeloma cells by simultaneously binding tumor antigens (BCMA, GPRC5D) and CD3 on T cells. Approved for relapsed/refractory disease.
Disease-Modifying Therapies (DMTs): Medications that can slow the progression of the disease and reduce the frequency and severity of relapses.
Symptomatic Treatments: Range of therapies to manage symptoms like spasticity, pain, fatigue, and bladder issues.
Physical Therapy and Rehabilitation: Exercise programs and physical therapy to maintain mobility, strength, and function.
Corticosteroids: Used to reduce inflammation and speed recovery during acute exacerbations.
Hearing Aid: Hearing aids for conductive hearing loss from stapes fixation.
Stapedectomy: Surgical replacement of the fixed stapes to restore hearing.
Orthopedic Management: Occupational therapy and adaptive devices for joint stiffness and limited range of motion.
Fludrocortisone: Mineralocorticoid to treat neurogenic orthostatic hypotension by increasing blood volume and sodium retention.
Midodrine: Alpha-1 adrenergic agonist for neurogenic orthostatic hypotension; increases peripheral vascular resistance.
Levodopa: Dopaminergic therapy for parkinsonian features; only ~30% of MSA-P patients show a clinically meaningful response, and response is typically transient.
Droxidopa: Norepinephrine precursor for neurogenic orthostatic hypotension; converts to norepinephrine in peripheral tissues.
Anticholinergics: Antimuscarinic medications for overactive bladder symptoms in MSA, used with caution when impaired bladder emptying is present.
Clean Intermittent Catheterization: Intermittent catheterization for urinary retention or difficult bladder emptying due to autonomic dysfunction.
Physical Therapy: Gait training, balance exercises, and fall prevention programs.
Speech and Language Therapy: Exercises for dysarthria management and swallowing evaluation/therapy for dysphagia.
Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG): Used to modulate the immune response and reduce inflammation.
Corticosteroids: Anti-inflammatory medicines to reduce immune system activity and inflammation.
Aspirin: Used in cases with high risk of blood clots to prevent thrombotic complications.
Supportive Care: Including fluids, blood pressure support, and respiratory support as needed.
Pacing: Activity management strategy to stay within energy limits and avoid triggering post-exertional malaise.
Symptomatic Treatment: Treatment of individual symptoms including sleep disturbance, pain, and orthostatic intolerance.
Low-Dose Naltrexone: Off-label use targeting TRPM3 ion channel dysfunction and neuroinflammation.
Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors: First-line symptomatic treatment (pyridostigmine).
Corticosteroids: Prednisone for immunosuppression, mainstay of therapy.
Thymectomy: Surgical removal of thymus, improves outcomes in appropriate patients.
Azathioprine: Steroid-sparing immunosuppressant.
Mycophenolate Mofetil: Alternative immunosuppressant.
Rituximab: Anti-CD20 antibody, effective in refractory cases.
Eculizumab: Complement inhibitor (anti-C5), approved for refractory AChR+ MG.
Plasma Exchange: Rapid removal of antibodies, used for myasthenic crisis.
Intravenous Immunoglobulin: Immunomodulation, used for exacerbations and crisis.
Pharmacotherapy for Myotonia: Mexiletine is the most commonly used antimyotonia agent. Other options include phenytoin and carbamazepine for symptomatic relief of myotonia.

Cardiac Monitoring and Pacemaker: Regular cardiac monitoring with ECG and Holter is essential. Pacemaker or implantable cardioverter-defibrillator may be required for conduction defects or arrhythmias.
Supportive Care: Multidisciplinary care including physical therapy, speech therapy, respiratory support (non-invasive ventilation), and ophthalmologic surveillance.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling is essential given autosomal dominant inheritance with anticipation. Congenital DM1 is almost exclusively maternally transmitted.

Carglumic acid (N-carbamyl-L-glutamate): Carglumic acid is a synthetic stable analogue of N-acetylglutamate that directly activates CPS1, bypassing deficient NAGS and restoring urea cycle flux. It is the specific disease-modifying therapy for NAGS deficiency and the only urea cycle disorder that can be cured by substitutive drug therapy. All patients in the 2024 case series responded well to carbamylglutamate therapy, with normalization of ammonia, citrulline, and orotic acid.

Dietary protein management: Although protein restriction was historically used, carbamylglutamate therapy allows most patients to liberalize their diets. However, protein restriction remains an important strategy during illness or disruption of medication access. The maximum safe level of protein intake to prevent hyperammonemia is not yet known.

Nitrogen scavenger therapy: Sodium benzoate, sodium phenylacetate, or sodium phenylbutyrate may be used as adjunctive ammonia-lowering agents during acute hyperammonemic crises. These drugs provide alternative nitrogen disposal pathways independent of the urea cycle.

Extracorporeal ammonia removal: Hemodialysis or continuous venovenous hemofiltration (CVVH) may be required for emergency ammonia removal during severe hyperammonemic crises, particularly when ammonia levels exceed 200 micromol/L or when pharmacological measures are insufficient.

Supportive care during acute crises: Emergency management of hyperammonemic crises includes cessation of protein intake, reversal of catabolism via high-calorie glucose infusion, correction of dehydration and electrolyte imbalances, and intensive care monitoring. Plans for illness and medication access disruption should be established for all patients.

Citrulline and arginine supplementation: Supplementation with citrulline or arginine has been reported in some cases of NAGS deficiency to support urea cycle intermediate availability. Arginine is also a positive regulator of NAGS enzyme activity in patients with residual enzyme function.

Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling is essential for affected families given the autosomal recessive inheritance, recurrence risk of 25% for each subsequent pregnancy, and availability of carrier testing and prenatal diagnosis. Molecular confirmation is also critical because NAGS deficiency is clinically indistinguishable from CPS1 deficiency yet has fundamentally different therapeutic implications.

Carbamylglutamate therapeutic trial: A therapeutic trial of carbamylglutamate is recommended for any patient with unexplained hyperammonemia and a proximal urea cycle defect pattern, as it can serve both as a diagnostic tool and as definitive treatment for NAGS deficiency.
Supportive care: Management is primarily supportive, including ventilatory support, anticonvulsant therapy, and nutritional supplementation. No disease-modifying therapy is currently available.

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Lipoic acid supplementation (ineffective): Lipoic acid supplementation has been attempted in LIPT2 patients and in yeast models but proved ineffective, consistent with the inability of exogenous lipoate to be incorporated into the mitochondrial lipoylation pathway in mammals.

payload.disease: Neonatal Severe Encephalopathy with Lactic Acidosis and Brain Abnormalities

text: Intensive Chemotherapy (7+3): Standard induction with 7 days cytarabine and 3 days anthracycline achieves high complete remission rates in NPM1-mutated AML. Consolidation with high-dose cytarabine is particularly effective for this subtype.

Venetoclax plus Azacitidine: For older or unfit patients, the combination of BCL2 inhibitor venetoclax with hypomethylating agent azacitidine achieves high response rates in NPM1-mutated AML, particularly in the absence of adverse features.

Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplantation: Generally reserved for patients with NPM1 mutation plus FLT3-ITD or other adverse features in first remission, or for patients who relapse. May not be necessary for favorable-risk NPM1-mutated AML in first remission.
MRD-Guided Therapy: NPM1 mutation transcript levels by RT-PCR provide sensitive minimal residual disease monitoring. Rising MRD levels can guide preemptive treatment before overt relapse.

MEK Inhibitors: MEK inhibitors (binimetinib, trametinib) have demonstrated modest activity in NRAS-mutant melanoma. Response rates are lower than in BRAF-mutant disease (approximately 15-20%), but MEK inhibition represents one of the few targeted options for this molecular subtype.

Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors: Anti-PD-1 antibodies (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) alone or combined with anti-CTLA-4 (ipilimumab) are the primary treatment for advanced NRAS-mutant melanoma. NRAS-mutant tumors may have higher tumor mutational burden, potentially enhancing immunotherapy responsiveness.

Combination Targeted Therapy: Clinical trials are investigating combinations targeting both MAPK and PI3K pathways, as well as CDK4/6 inhibitors given the importance of cell cycle dysregulation in NRAS-mutant melanoma.

Larotrectinib: Highly selective TRK inhibitor approved for adult and pediatric solid tumors with NTRK gene fusions. Demonstrates remarkable efficacy across tumor types with overall response rate of approximately 75% and durable responses. First tumor-agnostic TRK inhibitor approval.

Entrectinib: TRK inhibitor with additional activity against ROS1 and ALK. Approved for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors in adults and children. Penetrates CNS, providing activity against brain metastases.

Repotrectinib: Next-generation TRK inhibitor designed to overcome resistance mutations including solvent front mutations (G595R, G623R). Option for patients who progress on first-generation TRK inhibitors.
Sodium Oxybate: First-line for cataplexy and daytime sleepiness, improves nighttime sleep.
Modafinil: Wake-promoting agent for excessive daytime sleepiness.
Pitolisant: Histamine H3 receptor antagonist, promotes wakefulness.
Solriamfetol: Dopamine/norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor for sleepiness.
Amphetamines: Traditional stimulants (amphetamine, methylphenidate).
Antidepressants: SNRIs/SSRIs for cataplexy (venlafaxine, fluoxetine).
Scheduled Naps: Short planned naps can improve alertness.

Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy: NPC is highly radiosensitive. IMRT is the standard of care for locoregional disease, allowing precise dose delivery while sparing critical structures including parotid glands, temporal lobes, and brainstem.

Concurrent Chemoradiation: Concurrent cisplatin during radiation followed by adjuvant chemotherapy is standard for locally advanced NPC (stages III-IVA). This approach improves overall survival compared to radiation alone.
Induction Chemotherapy: Induction chemotherapy (gemcitabine-cisplatin or TPF) before chemoradiation is increasingly used for locally advanced disease based on recent trials showing improved survival.

Immunotherapy: PD-1 inhibitors have shown significant activity in recurrent/metastatic NPC, with durable responses in a subset of patients. EBV antigens may serve as targets for immunotherapy, and adoptive T cell therapy targeting EBV is under investigation.

Respiratory Support: Nocturnal non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) or continuous ventilatory support for respiratory muscle weakness. In NEB-NM, 55% of patients require ventilatory support with progressive need over time.
Physical Therapy: Ongoing physical therapy and rehabilitation to maintain mobility, prevent contractures, and optimize functional capacity.
Nutritional Support: Gastrostomy tube feeding for patients with severe bulbar weakness and failure to thrive. In NEB-NM, 32% of patients require gastrostomy.
Orthopedic Management: Management of scoliosis (bracing or surgical correction), foot deformities, and joint contractures.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for families to understand inheritance patterns, recurrence risk, and genetic testing options.

Fast Skeletal Muscle Troponin Activators (Investigational): Tirasemtiv and related fast skeletal muscle troponin activators increase submaximal muscle tension in preclinical NM models by enhancing thin filament calcium sensitivity. Improved respiratory efficiency observed in NEM3 mouse models.

Myosin Activators (Investigational): Omecamtiv mecarbil, a small-molecule cardiac myosin activator, substantially increased submaximal tension in type 1 muscle fibers from NEB-NM patients, with the largest effects in those with the lowest nebulin levels.
Renal Replacement Therapy: Dialysis or kidney transplantation for end-stage renal disease.
Antihypertensive Therapy: Management of blood pressure with medications.
Supportive Care: Symptomatic treatment and monitoring to manage associated complications.
Supportive topical skin care: Emollients and topical anti-inflammatory therapy remain baseline management for scaling and eczematous inflammation, though control is often incomplete.

Ex vivo SPINK5 gene-corrected epidermal sheet grafting (investigational): Phase I ex vivo lentiviral gene therapy strategy using autologous epidermal sheets generated from genetically modified skin stem cells to restore LEKTI in Netherton syndrome.

payload.treatment_name: Ex vivo SPINK5 gene-corrected epidermal sheet grafting (investigational)

text: Cytokine-targeted systemic pharmacotherapy: Biologics and JAK inhibitors are emerging options in severe disease, targeting IL-4/IL-13, IL-17, IL-36, and JAK pathways.
Immunoglobulin and biologic systemic therapy: Systematic review data indicate skin improvement in many reported cases with immunoglobulins and biologics.

Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) for infection-prone pediatric NS: High-dose IVIG has been used in children with recurrent skin/systemic infections and severe inflammatory skin disease, with reported improvement in skin status and infectious exacerbations.

payload.treatment_name: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) for infection-prone pediatric NS

text: Dupilumab (IL-4/IL-13 pathway inhibition): Dupilumab has shown improvement in pruritus and inflammatory skin disease in pediatric and mixed-age NS case reports/series.
Secukinumab (IL-17 pathway inhibition): IL-17A blockade with secukinumab has shown clinical improvement in refractory NS in case-level evidence.
Abrocitinib (JAK1 inhibitor): JAK1 inhibition with abrocitinib has shown symptomatic and severity improvements in case-level NS evidence.
Secukinumab and dupilumab combination (investigational/observational): Combination IL-17A and IL-4/IL-13 blockade has been reported in severe pediatric NS cohorts.

Risk-Adapted Chemotherapy: Chemotherapy intensity varies by risk group. Low-risk patients may need only observation or minimal chemotherapy. High-risk patients receive intensive induction chemotherapy including cisplatin, etoposide, vincristine, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and topotecan.

Surgical Resection: Surgery for primary tumor resection is performed after chemotherapy for high-risk disease. Complete resection is associated with better outcomes when achievable without significant morbidity.

Autologous Stem Cell Transplant: High-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell rescue is standard for high-risk neuroblastoma following induction chemotherapy and surgical resection. Tandem transplant may provide additional benefit.
Radiation Therapy: Radiation to the primary tumor bed is used in high-risk disease following surgical resection and stem cell transplant.

Anti-GD2 Immunotherapy: Anti-GD2 monoclonal antibody (dinutuximab) combined with GM-CSF and IL-2 has significantly improved outcomes in high-risk neuroblastoma during maintenance therapy. GD2 is highly expressed on neuroblastoma cells.
Isotretinoin (Retinoic Acid): 13-cis-retinoic acid (isotretinoin) is given during maintenance therapy to promote differentiation of residual neuroblastoma cells and reduce relapse risk.

MEK Inhibitors: Selumetinib is FDA-approved for symptomatic, inoperable plexiform neurofibromas in children aged 2 years and older. As a MEK inhibitor, it targets the downstream effector of hyperactive RAS signaling. Shrinkage of plexiform neurofibromas observed in clinical trials.

Surgical Management: Surgery for symptomatic neurofibromas causing pain, functional impairment, or disfigurement. Complete resection of plexiform neurofibromas often impossible due to infiltrative nature. Surgery is primary treatment for operable MPNST.

Surveillance Protocol: Annual clinical evaluation including skin, skeletal, neurologic, and ophthalmologic examination. Annual ophthalmologic evaluation in children (optic glioma screening). MRI for symptomatic lesions or suspected MPNST. Blood pressure monitoring for renovascular disease or pheochromocytoma.

Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for affected individuals and at-risk family members. Children of affected parents have 50% risk. High rate of de novo mutations means negative family history does not exclude NF1. Variable expressivity makes predicting severity difficult.
Eculizumab: Maintenance therapy approved for AQP-4 seropositive NMOSD.
Inebilizumab: Maintenance therapy approved for AQP-4 seropositive NMOSD.
Satralizumab: Maintenance therapy approved for AQP-4 seropositive NMOSD.
Acute Attack Treatment - Corticosteroids: High-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (1g daily for 3-5 days) is first-line therapy for acute attacks.
Acute Attack Treatment - Plasma Exchange: Plasmapheresis is used for severe attacks unresponsive to steroids, removing pathogenic antibodies from circulation.
Rituximab: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that depletes B cells. Widely used as maintenance therapy to prevent relapses.
Eculizumab: Complement C5 inhibitor approved for AQP4-positive NMOSD. Prevents complement-mediated astrocyte destruction by blocking terminal complement activation.
Inebilizumab: Anti-CD19 monoclonal antibody approved for AQP4-positive NMOSD. Provides broader B cell depletion than rituximab by targeting CD19.
Satralizumab: IL-6 receptor inhibitor approved for AQP4-positive NMOSD. IL-6 promotes plasmablast survival and antibody production.
Cerliponase Alfa: Enzyme replacement therapy used in CLN2 disease that slows decline in motor and language function.
Supportive Care: Palliative and symptomatic management remains the main treatment approach for most NCL subtypes.

Miglustat: Substrate reduction therapy approved in Europe and several other countries for treatment of neurological manifestations. Inhibits glucosylceramide synthase to reduce glycosphingolipid accumulation.

Intrathecal 2-Hydroxypropyl-Beta-Cyclodextrin: HPbCD given by lumbar intrathecal injection mobilizes cholesterol from lysosomes. Phase 1-2 trial showed slowed neurological disease progression compared to natural history.
Levacetylleucine: FDA-approved standalone treatment for NPC.
Arimoclomol: Co-inducer of heat shock proteins, approved for use in combination with miglustat for NPC. Enhances cellular stress response and may improve protein folding and lysosomal function.
Supportive Care: Multidisciplinary management including neurology, physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, nutrition support, and seizure management.
Genetic Counseling: Family screening, carrier testing, and prenatal/preimplantation genetic testing for at-risk relatives.
Surgery: Lobectomy or pneumonectomy for localized disease
Chemotherapy: Platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin or carboplatin) in combination with other agents
Targeted Therapy: Drugs targeting specific mutations, such as EGFR inhibitors (erlotinib, gefitinib), ALK inhibitors (crizotinib, ceritinib), and ROS1 inhibitors (crizotinib)
Immunotherapy: Checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) for tumors with high PD-L1 expression
Radiation Therapy: Used for localized disease, palliation of symptoms, or brain metastases

Sodium benzoate therapy: Sodium benzoate is the mainstay of glycine reduction therapy in NKH. Benzoate conjugates with glycine and is excreted in urine as hippurate, thereby lowering plasma glycine. Clinical targets commonly aim for plasma glycine approximately 120-300 umol/L.

Ketogenic diet: The ketogenic diet is used as an alternative glycine-lowering strategy. In a multi-center series, six infants switching from high-dose benzoate to KD with low-dose benzoate showed approximately 28% lower plasma glycine, reduced brain glycine by MRS, and seizure reduction in half of patients. KD promotes glycine use as a gluconeogenic substrate.

NMDA antagonist therapy (dextromethorphan/ketamine): NMDA receptor antagonists such as dextromethorphan and ketamine have been used to counteract presumed NMDA receptor overactivation by excess glycine. However, a 2024 expert reassessment finds insufficient evidence of added therapeutic benefit beyond glycine reduction strategies, and notes potential adverse behavioral effects. Controlled clinical studies have never been performed.

Anticonvulsant therapy: Antiepileptic drugs are used for seizure management in NKH. Sodium valproate is specifically contraindicated because it increases both CSF and plasma glycine levels, potentially worsening seizures. Appropriate anticonvulsant selection is critical in NKH management.

Supportive care: Comprehensive supportive care including ventilatory support for apnea, feeding support, developmental therapies, and monitoring for metabolic decompensation. NKH neonates frequently require intensive care. Long-term care includes management of neurodevelopmental disability.

Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling is indicated for families with NKH given the autosomal recessive inheritance, 25% recurrence risk, and the availability of molecular genetic testing. Prenatal diagnosis is possible when familial variants are known.

Growth Hormone Therapy: Recombinant human growth hormone (rhGH) is FDA-approved for treatment of short stature in Noonan syndrome. Can improve final adult height by approximately 1 standard deviation.
Cardiac Surgical Intervention: Balloon valvuloplasty or surgical valvotomy for pulmonary valve stenosis; septal myectomy or alcohol ablation for severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

MEK Inhibitor Therapy: Trametinib (MEK1/2 inhibitor) has shown promising results in case reports for treatment-refractory hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and lymphatic complications. MEK inhibition can reverse cardiomyocyte hypertrophy in animal models.
Early Intervention Services: Developmental support including speech therapy, physical therapy, occupational therapy, and special education services for developmental delays and learning disabilities.
Speech Therapy: Speech and language therapy for articulation difficulties and language delays.
Physical Therapy: Physical therapy to address motor delays and hypotonia common in Noonan syndrome.
Lifestyle Modification: Dietary changes and increased physical activity as foundation of treatment.
GLP-1 Receptor Agonists: Semaglutide, liraglutide provide substantial weight loss through central appetite suppression and metabolic effects.
GLP-1/GIP Dual Agonists: Tirzepatide offers enhanced weight loss of 15-25% through dual incretin receptor activation.
Bariatric Surgery: Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, sleeve gastrectomy for severe obesity.
Behavioral Therapy: Cognitive behavioral therapy for eating behaviors.
Orlistat: Lipase inhibitor reducing fat absorption.

Dietary and lifestyle intervention: Nutrition, physical activity, and behavioral interventions remain the foundation of treatment across the spectrum, although monogenic and hypothalamic forms are often refractory to lifestyle measures alone.

payload.disease: Obesity Due to MC4R Pathway Disruption

text: Metreleptin replacement: Recombinant leptin replacement is the mechanism-matched therapy for congenital leptin deficiency and may require variant-aware dosing in antagonistic leptin forms. This treatment is specific to the LEP subtype.

payload.disease: Obesity Due to MC4R Pathway Disruption

text: Setmelanotide: MC4R agonist therapy that bypasses several upstream defects in the pathway and can reduce hunger and body weight in selected patients with monogenic, syndromic, and some receptor-level MC4R pathway disorders.

payload.disease: Obesity Due to MC4R Pathway Disruption

text: GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy: GLP-1 receptor agonists provide a non-hypothalamic satiety signal and are an emerging treatment strategy for hypothalamic obesity, especially acquired cases after suprasellar tumors or other hypothalamic injury.

payload.disease: Obesity Due to MC4R Pathway Disruption

text: CPAP: Continuous positive airway pressure, gold standard treatment.
BiPAP: Bilevel positive airway pressure for some patients.
Oral Appliances: Mandibular advancement devices for mild-moderate OSA.
Weight Loss: Can significantly reduce or resolve OSA.
Positional Therapy: Avoiding supine sleep position.
Surgery: UPPP, maxillomandibular advancement for selected patients.
Hypoglossal Nerve Stimulation: Implantable device for CPAP-intolerant patients.
Orthopedic surgical management: Surgical intervention for management of radial head dislocation and other skeletal deformities. Limb-lengthening procedures may be considered in severe cases.
Physical therapy: Rehabilitation to maintain and improve joint range of motion, particularly at the elbows and knees where restricted extension is common.
Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling for families, particularly to distinguish between the autosomal recessive and autosomal dominant forms and to inform recurrence risk assessment.
Ivermectin therapy: Ivermectin is the drug of choice for skin and ocular manifestations.

Bisphosphonate therapy: Intravenous pamidronate has improved bone mineral density and gross motor function in patients with opsismodysplasia. Treatment benefit has been demonstrated both in patients with and without hypophosphatemic rickets.
Phosphate and calcitriol supplementation: Oral phosphorus and calcitriol supplementation for patients with renal phosphate wasting and hypophosphatemic rickets.
Protein-restricted diet: Dietary nitrogen control tailored to age and growth requirements to reduce ammonia production.
Nitrogen scavenger therapy: Pharmacologic ammonia-scavenging agents (sodium benzoate, sodium phenylbutyrate, glycerol phenylbutyrate) for chronic and acute management.
Arginine supplementation: L-arginine supplementation to provide substrate for the urea cycle downstream of the OTC block.

Acute hyperammonemia management: Rapid crisis-directed supportive management including protein restriction, glucose infusion, nitrogen scavengers, and emergent hemodialysis when ammonia exceeds 200 micromol/L.
Liver transplantation: Curative metabolic option for selected severe phenotypes; restores full urea cycle capacity and eliminates hyperammonemic risk.
Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling for X-linked inheritance pattern, carrier testing in at-risk females, and prenatal/preconception planning.
Surgical Correction of Oral Anomalies: Surgical removal of accessory frenula, tongue hamartomas, and repair of cleft lip/palate as needed.
Renal Management: Monitoring and management of polycystic kidney disease, including dialysis or transplantation for progressive renal failure.
Neurodevelopmental Support: Early intervention and educational support for intellectual disability and developmental delay.
Exercise: Regular exercise and strengthening are core first-line treatments to improve pain and function.
Weight Loss: Weight reduction is recommended for overweight or obese patients with hip or knee osteoarthritis.
NSAIDs: Topical or oral NSAIDs are standard symptom-directed pharmacologic therapy when not contraindicated.
Intra-articular Glucocorticoid Injections: Intra-articular corticosteroid injections provide short-term symptom relief, particularly for knee osteoarthritis flares.
Total Joint Replacement: Total hip or knee arthroplasty is an effective late-stage option for advanced symptoms with structural damage.
Bisphosphonate Therapy: Bisphosphonates (pamidronate, zoledronic acid) are used to increase bone mineral density and reduce fracture frequency, particularly in children.
Physical Therapy: Exercise and physical therapy to strengthen muscles and improve mobility while protecting fragile bones.
Fracture Management: Standard orthopedic management of fractures with appropriate immobilization.
Supportive Care: Palliative supportive care given the lethal nature of the condition. Focus on comfort and family support.
Bisphosphonate Therapy: Intravenous bisphosphonates (pamidronate, zoledronic acid) to increase bone density and reduce fracture frequency. Most beneficial in childhood.
Rodding Surgery: Intramedullary rodding (telescoping rods) to stabilize long bones, correct deformities, and prevent fractures. Multiple surgeries typically required as the child grows.
Spinal Fusion: Surgical stabilization of severe scoliosis to prevent respiratory compromise.
Physical Therapy: Careful physical therapy to maintain mobility and muscle strength while minimizing fracture risk.
Bisphosphonate Therapy: Bisphosphonates can improve vertebral bone density and reduce some fracture burden, particularly in childhood.
Orthopedic Management: Fracture treatment and corrective surgery for deformities as needed. Intramedullary rodding for severe cases.
Physical Therapy: Physical therapy and rehabilitation to optimize mobility, walking capacity, and overall function.
Bisphosphonate Therapy: Bisphosphonates may reduce fracture frequency, though hyperplastic callus can still occur.
Surgical Management: Orthopedic surgery for fractures and deformities. Care must be taken regarding hyperplastic callus formation after procedures.
Interosseous Membrane Excision: Surgical removal of calcified interosseous membrane to restore forearm rotation in selected cases.
Bisphosphonate Therapy: Bisphosphonates to improve bone density, though response may differ from classical OI due to distinct pathophysiology.
Orthopedic Management: Fracture management and corrective surgery for deformities. Rodding procedures for long bone stabilization.
Physical Therapy: Careful rehabilitation to maintain mobility while minimizing fracture risk.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for families regarding recurrence risk and carrier testing for at-risk relatives.
Orthopedic management: Orthopedic management of skeletal complications including rhizomelic dwarfism and fibrous bone lesions. Surgical intervention may be required for severe limb deformities.
Craniofacial surgery: Surgical correction of craniosynostosis and midface hypoplasia as needed.

Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation: HSCT is the only curative treatment for osteoclast-rich forms of ARO. Donor-derived monocyte-macrophage precursors differentiate into functional osteoclasts that restore bone resorption. Best outcomes are achieved with early transplantation before irreversible complications develop. HSCT is not effective for osteoclast-poor forms (TNFSF11 mutations) or for ADO2.

Supportive care and monitoring: For non-infantile forms where HSCT is not indicated, management is supportive. Includes monitoring for cranial nerve impingement, anemia, dental disease, and mineral metabolism abnormalities. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation may be needed for hypocalcemia.
Bisphosphonates: First-line antiresorptives (alendronate, risedronate, zoledronic acid).
Denosumab: RANKL inhibitor, potent antiresorptive.
Teriparatide: PTH analog, anabolic agent for severe osteoporosis.
Romosozumab: Sclerostin inhibitor with dual anabolic/antiresorptive effect.
Calcium Supplementation: Ensure adequate intake (1000-1200 mg/day).
Vitamin D Supplementation: Maintain 25(OH)D >30 ng/mL.
Weight-Bearing Exercise: Helps maintain bone density.
Surgical Resection: Surgical removal of the primary tumor when feasible.
Multi-Agent Chemotherapy: Combination chemotherapy used in neoadjuvant and adjuvant settings.

Aural toilet and debridement: Thorough mechanical debridement of fungal debris from the external auditory canal is the cornerstone of treatment and essential for topical antifungal efficacy. The most common treatment modality used in clinical practice.

Topical antifungal therapy: Topical clotrimazole is the most commonly used antifungal agent. Other options include miconazole and nystatin. Systemic triazoles (fluconazole, itraconazole) are reserved for severe or refractory disease.

Acidifying agents: Acidifying agents such as 2% acetic acid or boric acid alcohol solutions are used as alternatives to antifungals, particularly for mild cases or antifungal-resistant species. They restore the acidic pH of the ear canal that inhibits fungal growth.
Orthopedic Surgery: Surgical treatment for hand and foot malformations, scoliosis bracing and surgical intervention, and correction of orthognathic deformities.
Hearing Aids: Hearing aids for conductive hearing loss, with annual audiology evaluation recommended.
Respiratory Support: Continuous positive airway pressure and mandibular distraction for airway complications. Chest expansion surgery for thoracic hypoplasia.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling regarding X-linked inheritance, male lethality in severe subtypes, and prenatal testing options.

Platinum-Based Chemotherapy: Carboplatin-paclitaxel combination is the standard first-line chemotherapy for HGSOC. Platinum sensitivity is a key prognostic factor, with initial response rates of 70-80%. Approximately 25% of patients develop platinum resistance within 6 months.

PARP Inhibitor Therapy: PARP inhibitors (olaparib, niraparib, rucaparib) exploit synthetic lethality in HRD-positive tumors. In the SOLO1 trial, maintenance olaparib after platinum-based chemotherapy showed a hazard ratio of 0.55 for overall survival at 7-year follow-up in BRCA-mutant patients, with 67% alive versus 46.5% on placebo.

Bevacizumab: Bevacizumab, an anti-VEGF monoclonal antibody, is used in combination with chemotherapy and as maintenance therapy. The GOG-0218 validation study showed that patients with poorly chemosensitive disease (unfavorable KELIM score) derive the most benefit from bevacizumab.

Debulking Surgery: Primary or interval cytoreductive surgery aims to remove all visible tumor (complete gross resection). Optimal debulking to no residual disease is one of the strongest prognostic factors in HGSOC.

Alpelisib: PI3K alpha-selective inhibitor FDA-approved for HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer with PIK3CA mutation, in combination with fulvestrant, after progression on endocrine therapy. SOLAR-1 trial demonstrated significant PFS benefit. Key toxicity is hyperglycemia due to PI3K role in insulin signaling.

Endocrine Therapy Plus CDK4/6 Inhibitors: First-line treatment for metastatic ER+/HER2- disease typically combines aromatase inhibitors with CDK4/6 inhibitors, regardless of PIK3CA status. Alpelisib is used after progression on this regimen.

Capivasertib: AKT inhibitor that has shown activity in PIK3CA-mutated, AKT1-mutated, or PTEN-altered breast cancer. Approved in combination with fulvestrant for HR+/HER2- advanced breast cancer after prior endocrine therapy.
Beta-Blocker Therapy: Beta-blockers (sotalol, metoprolol) are first-line antiarrhythmic therapy to suppress ventricular arrhythmias and reduce adrenergic stimulation.

Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator: ICD implantation is the primary intervention for prevention of sudden cardiac death in high-risk patients with ARVC, including those with sustained ventricular arrhythmias, syncope, or significant RV dysfunction.

Exercise Restriction: Avoidance of competitive and high-intensity endurance exercise is recommended as vigorous exercise accelerates disease progression by increasing right ventricular wall stress and promoting desmosomal disruption.
Antiarrhythmic Drug Therapy: Antiarrhythmic agents (amiodarone, flecainide) may be used adjunctively for suppression of ventricular arrhythmias when beta-blockers are insufficient.

Heart Transplantation: Cardiac transplantation is indicated for end-stage biventricular failure refractory to medical therapy. Combined endpoint of death or heart transplant was less frequent in PKP2 carriers than non-PKP2 ARVC patients.
S-Adenosylmethionine Supplementation: SAM supplementation has shown therapeutic promise in Arts syndrome patients, suggesting it can compensate for PRS-I deficiency and alleviate symptoms.
Hearing Rehabilitation: Hearing aids or cochlear implants for sensorineural hearing loss.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling regarding X-linked recessive inheritance and carrier testing for at-risk females.
Urate-Lowering Therapy: Allopurinol or other xanthine oxidase inhibitors to reduce uric acid production and prevent gout and nephrolithiasis.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling regarding X-linked inheritance.
Vismodegib: Oral SMO inhibitor that lowers new and existing BCC burden in PTCH1-related disease, with tolerability constraints during chronic use.

payload.disease: PTCH1-related nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome

text: Sonidegib: Alternative SMO inhibitor for advanced BCC in patients requiring pathway-targeted therapy.

payload.disease: PTCH1-related nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome

text: Surgical Excision: Repeated lesion-directed surgery remains central for local disease control.

payload.disease: PTCH1-related nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome

text: Sunlight Avoidance: Strict UV protection reduces new BCC development.

payload.disease: PTCH1-related nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome

text: Genetic Counseling: Family counseling and cascade testing are recommended because of autosomal dominant transmission.

payload.disease: PTCH1-related nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome

text: Surgical Correction of Polydactyly: Surgical removal of extra digits in early childhood for functional and cosmetic improvement.
Anal Atresia Repair: Neonatal surgical correction of imperforate anus, typically posterior sagittal anorectoplasty (PSARP).
Endocrine Management: Monitoring and treatment of precocious puberty secondary to hypothalamic hamartoma. GnRH analogs may be used if central precocious puberty develops.

Surgical Resection (Whipple Procedure): Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) is the only potentially curative treatment for PDAC but is feasible in only 15-20% of patients at diagnosis. R0 resection followed by adjuvant chemotherapy provides the best long-term survival outcomes.

FOLFIRINOX Chemotherapy: Combination of fluorouracil, leucovorin, irinotecan, and oxaliplatin. Used as first-line treatment for metastatic PDAC in fit patients and as adjuvant therapy after surgical resection. Provides superior survival compared to gemcitabine alone but with greater toxicity.

Gemcitabine-Based Chemotherapy: Gemcitabine monotherapy or in combination with nab-paclitaxel is a standard treatment for advanced PDAC. Gemcitabine plus nab-paclitaxel provides improved survival over gemcitabine alone and is better tolerated than FOLFIRINOX.
PARP Inhibitor Therapy: Olaparib is approved as maintenance therapy for BRCA-mutated metastatic PDAC that has not progressed on first-line platinum-based chemotherapy, based on the POLO trial.

Surgical Resection: Surgical resection (typically distal pancreatectomy) is the standard treatment for MCNs >=40 mm or those with risk features (mural nodule) or symptoms, per 2018 European guidelines. Complete resection is curative for non-invasive MCNs. Partial resection should be avoided due to the risk of adenoma-carcinoma progression. MCNs <40 mm without risk features may be safely surveilled.

Surveillance for Malignancy: Postoperative surveillance is recommended after resection of MCNs, particularly for those with higher-grade dysplasia or invasive components, to monitor for recurrence. For unresected MCNs <40mm without risk features, surveillance with MRI every 6 months for the first year then annually is recommended.

Surgical Resection: Surgery is the only curative treatment for localized pNETs. Options include enucleation for small tumors, distal pancreatectomy, or pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) depending on tumor location and size.

Somatostatin Analog Therapy: Somatostatin analogs (octreotide, lanreotide) control hormonal symptoms in functional pNETs and have demonstrated antiproliferative effects in well-differentiated G1/G2 tumors. The CLARINET trial demonstrated that lanreotide significantly prolonged progression-free survival compared to placebo.

Everolimus (mTOR Inhibitor): Everolimus, an oral mTOR inhibitor, is approved for advanced progressive pNETs based on the RADIANT-3 trial. It demonstrated a 65% reduction in estimated risk of progression or death compared to placebo.

Sunitinib (Multi-Kinase Inhibitor): Sunitinib, a multi-targeted receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR, PDGFR, and c-KIT, is approved for progressive well-differentiated pNETs. The registration trial showed median PFS of 11.4 months vs 5.5 months with placebo.

PRRT (Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy): 177Lu-DOTATATE (Lutathera) targets somatostatin receptor-positive NETs. A retrospective study of 34 patients with metastatic functioning pNETs demonstrated partial or complete response in 59% and median PFS of 18.1 months, establishing PRRT as a safe and effective therapy for pNETs.

Temozolomide-Based Chemotherapy: Temozolomide alone or combined with capecitabine (CAPTEM) is used for advanced pNETs. The E2211 randomized trial showed median PFS of 22.7 months for CAPTEM versus 14.4 months for temozolomide alone, with the highest response rates reported in a randomized study for pancreatic NETs.
Levodopa/Carbidopa: Gold standard treatment, replaces dopamine precursor.
Dopamine Agonists: Directly stimulate dopamine receptors.
MAO-B Inhibitors: Prevent dopamine breakdown (selegiline, rasagiline).
COMT Inhibitors: Extend levodopa duration (entacapone).
Deep Brain Stimulation: Surgical therapy for advanced motor fluctuations.
Physical Therapy: Maintains mobility and reduces fall risk.
Corticosteroids: First-line treatment for reducing inflammation in pars planitis.
Immunosuppressive Agents: Second-line therapy for chronic or steroid-resistant cases.
Biological Therapy: TNF-alpha inhibitors for refractory cases.
Pars Plana Vitrectomy: Surgical intervention for severe vitreous opacities or complications.
Supportive Care: Includes rest, fluids, and analgesics for managing symptoms.
Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG): Used in severe cases, particularly for individuals with immunodeficiency or severe anemia.

Supportive Care: No cure exists for PMD. Treatment is supportive and includes physical therapy, occupational therapy, and management of spasticity with medications such as baclofen. Seizure management with antiepileptic drugs when needed.
Physical Therapy: Rehabilitation to maintain mobility, prevent contractures, and optimize functional abilities.

Stem Cell Transplantation (Investigational): Neural stem cell and glial progenitor cell transplantation are being investigated as potential therapies for PMD, aiming to provide donor-derived oligodendrocytes capable of myelinating host axons.

PLP1 Antisense Oligonucleotide Therapy (Investigational): Antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) targeting PLP1 mRNA to suppress expression have shown dramatic preclinical efficacy in the jimpy mouse model, fully restoring oligodendrocyte numbers, myelination, motor function, and lifespan. This approach exploits the observation that PLP1-null individuals have milder disease than those with gain-of-function mutations.
Corticosteroids: High-dose for induction.
Rituximab: First-line in combination with steroids per guidelines.
Azathioprine: Steroid-sparing maintenance agent.
Mycophenolate Mofetil: Alternative steroid-sparing agent.
Imatinib: First-line tyrosine kinase inhibitor used in Penttinen syndrome, targeting constitutively activated PDGFRB with partial clinical response.
Dasatinib: Second-generation tyrosine kinase inhibitor with greater potency against the Val665Ala variant than imatinib. Used when imatinib response is incomplete.
Smoking Cessation: Most important intervention, reduces progression.
Antiplatelet Therapy: Aspirin or clopidogrel to reduce cardiovascular events.
Statins: LDL lowering, plaque stabilization.
ACE Inhibitors: Cardiovascular risk reduction.
Supervised Exercise Therapy: First-line for claudication, improves walking distance.
Cilostazol: Phosphodiesterase inhibitor for claudication symptoms.
Revascularization: Endovascular or surgical for severe disease.

Antiepileptic Drug Therapy: Treatment of epilepsy generally follows principles for a seizure disorder caused by a known structural brain abnormality. Anti-seizure medication is typically selected based on teratogenic risk, tolerability, and efficacy.

Cardiovascular Surveillance: Regular cardiology evaluations with echocardiogram, stress testing, and cardiac MRI to monitor for aortic dilation, valvular dystrophy, and dissection risk. Good blood pressure control is recommended.

Lung Transplantation: Lung transplantation is a viable therapeutic option for infants with FLNA-associated progressive respiratory failure. Five of six patients survived with unrestricted lives post-transplant.

Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling regarding X-linked inheritance, high prenatal lethality in males, recurrence risk, and prenatal testing options. Evaluation of at-risk relatives for cardiovascular complications is recommended even in neurologically asymptomatic individuals.
Symptomatic Management: Supportive care addressing specific symptoms such as physical therapy for hypotonia and seizure management.
Nutritional Support: Specialized diet and supplements to manage biochemical abnormalities.
Liver Transplant: Considered in severe cases with significant liver dysfunction.
Genetic Counseling: Providing information and support to families regarding inheritance and implications.
Endoscopic polypectomy: Regular surveillance endoscopy with removal of polyps >10-15 mm reduces bleeding complications and cancer risk. Multiple sessions may be needed given the polyp burden.
Surgical resection: Reserved for cases with severe polyp burden, recurrent intussusception, or malignant transformation.
Genetic counseling: Family members should receive counseling and cascade genetic testing for STK11 mutations.
Cranial vault reconstruction: Surgical expansion of the cranial vault to relieve intracranial pressure and correct skull deformity. May require staged procedures depending on severity.
Midface advancement surgery: Le Fort III or monobloc osteotomy to advance the midface, improving airway, proptosis, and facial appearance.

Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor plus Chemotherapy: Addition of TKIs (imatinib, dasatinib) to multi-agent chemotherapy dramatically improves remission rates and survival in Ph+ ALL. Dasatinib achieves better CNS penetration. TKIs are continued through all phases of treatment including maintenance.
Ponatinib: Third-generation TKI with activity against T315I mutation. Used for patients with TKI-resistant mutations or as frontline therapy in combination with chemotherapy for enhanced efficacy.

Blinatumomab: CD19-CD3 bispecific T-cell engager antibody effective in relapsed/refractory Ph+ ALL. Can be combined with TKIs. Achieves molecular remissions in many patients, potentially enabling chemotherapy-free regimens.

Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplantation: Historically considered standard for all Ph+ ALL patients in first remission. With improved TKI-based regimens, transplant may be reserved for patients with persistent MRD or high-risk features. Provides graft-versus-leukemia effect.
CAR-T Cell Therapy: CD19-directed CAR-T cells are effective in relapsed/refractory Ph+ ALL. Can achieve durable remissions in patients who have failed TKIs and chemotherapy.

Speech and language therapy: Augmentative and alternative communication strategies are essential for individuals with absent or limited speech, including picture exchange systems, speech-generating devices, and sign language.
Behavioral therapy: Applied behavior analysis (ABA) and other behavioral interventions targeting autism spectrum features, adaptive skills, and challenging behaviors.
Antiepileptic medication: Seizure management with anticonvulsant medications tailored to the individual seizure type and response.

Insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) therapy: IGF-1 has been investigated as a targeted treatment for PMS. Preclinical studies showed that IGF-1 restores excitatory synaptic deficits in iPSC-derived PMS neurons. A pilot controlled trial in children showed improvement in social impairment and restrictive behaviors.
Phenylalanine-Restricted Diet: Lifelong dietary restriction of phenylalanine intake, mainstay of treatment.
Medical Formula: Phenylalanine-free amino acid supplements to provide protein needs.
Sapropterin (Kuvan): BH4 cofactor replacement for responsive patients, allows dietary liberalization.
Pegvaliase (Palynziq): Enzyme substitution therapy using PEGylated phenylalanine ammonia lyase.
Large Neutral Amino Acids: Compete with phenylalanine for brain transport, adjunctive therapy.

Surgical Resection: Complete surgical resection is the primary treatment for localized PPGL. Requires careful preoperative alpha-blockade followed by beta-blockade to prevent perioperative hypertensive crisis.
Alpha-Adrenergic Blockade: Preoperative alpha-blockade with phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin is essential to control hypertension and allow volume expansion before surgery.
MIBG Therapy: 131I-MIBG (metaiodobenzylguanidine) is a targeted radiotherapy for MIBG-avid metastatic PPGL. High-specific-activity 131I-MIBG is FDA-approved.
Temozolomide: Alkylating chemotherapy showing activity in metastatic PPGL, particularly SDHB-mutated tumors with MGMT promoter methylation.
Sunitinib: Multi-kinase inhibitor with antiangiogenic activity showing benefit in progressive metastatic PPGL. Used in clinical practice despite limited prospective data.
Symptomatic Management: Management focuses on addressing symptoms, such as using medications to manage behavior and support cognitive function.
Physical Therapy: Physical therapy to maintain mobility and manage motor symptoms that may emerge in later disease stages.
Occupational Therapy: Occupational therapy to support daily living activities and maintain independence as cognitive and behavioral symptoms progress.
Speech Therapy: Speech therapy for patients with language variant (primary progressive aphasia) to support communication abilities.
Caregiver Support: Education and resources for caregivers to manage progressive symptoms and provide quality care.
Penicillin injection: Single-dose intramuscular penicillin treats all stages of pinta.
Aminoglycoside Antibiotics: Streptomycin and gentamicin are first-line treatments for plague. Aminoglycosides have the longest track record of efficacy against Y. pestis infection.
Tetracycline Antibiotics: Doxycycline and other tetracyclines are effective alternatives for plague treatment and prophylaxis. Used when aminoglycosides are contraindicated.
Supportive Care: Intensive supportive care including fluid resuscitation, vasopressor support, and management of DIC complications for severe septicemic and pneumonic plague.
Albendazole therapy: Benzimidazole antiparasitic used for smaller or inoperable hydatid cysts and as adjunct around surgery.
Radical hepatic resection: Surgical removal of involved hepatic segments for large polycystic echinococcosis lesions or when malignancy is suspected.

Tolvaptan: Vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist that slows cyst growth and kidney enlargement in ADPKD. First disease-modifying therapy approved for ADPKD. Requires hepatic monitoring due to risk of elevated liver enzymes.
Blood Pressure Control: Aggressive hypertension management with ACE inhibitors or ARBs to slow disease progression and reduce cardiovascular risk.
Renal Dialysis: Dialysis (hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis) for end-stage renal disease. PKD is one of the leading causes of kidney failure requiring dialysis.
Kidney Transplantation: Kidney transplantation is the preferred treatment for end-stage renal disease in PKD. Native nephrectomy may be required if kidneys are too large.
Combined Oral Contraceptives: First-line for menstrual irregularity and hyperandrogenism.
Metformin: Improves insulin sensitivity and may restore ovulation.
Spironolactone: Anti-androgen for hirsutism and acne.
Letrozole: First-line ovulation induction for fertility.
Clomiphene Citrate: Alternative ovulation induction agent.
Lifestyle Modification: Weight loss improves all PCOS features.
Therapeutic Phlebotomy: Therapeutic phlebotomy to maintain hematocrit below 45% is the cornerstone of treatment for all PV patients. Reduces thrombotic risk by lowering blood viscosity.
Low-Dose Aspirin: Low-dose aspirin (81-100 mg daily) is recommended for all PV patients without contraindications to reduce thrombotic risk. Reduces arterial and possibly venous thrombosis.
Hydroxyurea: First-line cytoreductive therapy for high-risk PV patients (age >60 years or prior thrombosis). Reduces all three cell lines and decreases thrombotic events.

Ruxolitinib: JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor approved for PV inadequately controlled by hydroxyurea. Provides excellent control of hematocrit, reduces spleen size, and markedly improves symptom burden including pruritus and fatigue.
Interferon-alpha: Alternative to hydroxyurea, particularly for younger patients and during pregnancy. May induce molecular remissions with reduction in JAK2 V617F allele burden.
Corticosteroids: First-line therapy; high-dose initially with gradual taper.
Methotrexate: Most commonly used steroid-sparing agent.
Azathioprine: Alternative immunosuppressant for maintenance.
IVIG: For refractory cases or when immunosuppression contraindicated.

Enzyme replacement therapy (ERT): Intravenous infusion of recombinant human GAA is the standard of care for Pompe disease. Alglucosidase alfa (Myozyme/Lumizyme) was the first approved ERT (2006). Avalglucosidase alfa (Nexviazyme), a next-generation ERT with enhanced mannose-6-phosphate receptor binding, was approved in 2021 and shows improved skeletal muscle uptake. ERT significantly improves survival and cardiac outcomes in IOPD and stabilizes or improves respiratory and motor function in LOPD.

Respiratory support: Non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) or invasive mechanical ventilation may be required for respiratory insufficiency. Many LOPD patients require nocturnal ventilatory support, and some progress to continuous ventilator dependence.

Physical therapy and rehabilitation: Structured physical therapy and rehabilitation programs help maintain mobility, prevent contractures, and optimize residual muscle function. Exercise programs should be tailored to avoid overexertion.
Dietary management: High-protein diet may help preserve muscle mass. In IOPD, specialized feeding support including nasogastric or gastrostomy feeding may be needed.

Exercise Training: Graded exercise programs, particularly recumbent exercise initially, have been shown to improve cardiovascular conditioning, expand blood volume, and reduce orthostatic tachycardia in POTS patients. After 3 months of training, over 50% of patients no longer met criteria for POTS.
Volume Expansion and Salt Supplementation: Increased fluid intake (2-3 liters/day) and sodium supplementation (6-10 g/day) to expand plasma volume and improve venous return.
Pharmacotherapy - Beta-Blockers: Low-dose propranolol (20 mg) to attenuate the excessive heart rate response upon standing. Higher doses may worsen symptoms despite greater heart rate reduction.
Pharmacotherapy - Midodrine: Alpha-1 adrenergic agonist that promotes peripheral vasoconstriction, improving venous return and reducing orthostatic symptoms.
Pharmacotherapy - Fludrocortisone: Mineralocorticoid that promotes sodium and fluid retention to expand intravascular volume.
Compression Garments: Abdominal binders and lower extremity compression stockings to reduce venous pooling and improve venous return upon standing.

Antibiotic Therapy: First-line treatment with antibiotics such as ciprofloxacin and metronidazole to reduce bacterial overgrowth and restore microbial balance. Response rates are typically 80-90% for acute pouchitis.

Probiotic Therapy: VSL#3 (now marketed as Visbiome), a high-potency probiotic mixture containing 8 bacterial strains, has demonstrated efficacy in preventing recurrent pouchitis and maintaining remission.
Anti-inflammatory Medications: Topical or oral 5-aminosalicylic acid (5-ASA) compounds, topical corticosteroids, or corticosteroid enemas for management of chronic or refractory pouchitis.
Immunosuppressive Therapy: For chronic refractory pouchitis, biologic agents (anti-TNF therapy such as infliximab or adalimumab) or immunomodulators (azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine) may be necessary.

Growth Hormone Therapy: Recombinant human growth hormone is standard of care, improving growth, body composition, muscle strength, and exercise capacity. Typically initiated in infancy or early childhood. Meta-analysis shows significant improvements in height-SDS and BMI-SDS.

Dietary Management: Strict environmental control of food access is essential to prevent life-threatening obesity. Structured meal plans with caloric restriction combined with regular physical activity. Constant food security measures are necessary throughout life.
Hormone Replacement Therapy: Sex hormone replacement at puberty for development of secondary sexual characteristics. Testosterone for males, estrogen/progesterone for females.

Diazoxide Choline Extended-Release: K-ATP channel opener that reduces insulin secretion and modulates hypothalamic signaling. Shows improvements in hyperphagia scores, aggression, anxiety, and compulsivity in long-term open-label study.

Intranasal Oxytocin: Aimed at replacing deficient hypothalamic oxytocin. Double-blind crossover study showed trends toward improvement in social behavior but no statistically significant effects at day 6.

GLP-1 Receptor Agonists: Incretin-mediated appetite suppression using agents such as semaglutide and dulaglutide. Emerging as potential treatment for PWS-associated obesity and hyperphagia based on case reports and general pediatric obesity data. Clinical trials ongoing in PWS populations.

Gene Reactivation Strategies: Potentially curative approaches aiming to reactivate the silenced maternal copy of SNORD116 and other PWS-region genes. Strategies include antisense oligonucleotides targeting UBE3A-ATS, CRISPR epigenome editing with dCas9 fused to transcriptional activators, and small molecule epigenetic modulators. Currently in preclinical or early-phase development.

Genetic Counseling: Family screening and counseling regarding recurrence risk, which varies by genetic mechanism. Recurrence risk is typically low (<1%) for de novo deletions, ~1% for UPD, but up to 50% for imprinting center defects with microdeletions. Prenatal testing options available.
Ursodeoxycholic Acid (UDCA): First-line therapy, improves biochemistry and transplant-free survival.
Obeticholic Acid: Second-line for inadequate UDCA response.
Fibrates: Bezafibrate or fenofibrate as add-on therapy.
Liver Transplantation: For end-stage disease.

L-carnitine supplementation: Lifelong oral L-carnitine replacement is the disease-modifying cornerstone of PCD management. Standard dosing is 100-300 mg/kg/day in divided doses, titrated to plasma free carnitine levels and clinical response. Early treatment can reverse cardiomyopathy, with LVEF normalization reported within months.

Dietary management: Avoidance of prolonged fasting is essential to prevent metabolic decompensation. Frequent feeding schedules and carbohydrate-rich snacks before bed help maintain energy supply and reduce dependence on fatty acid oxidation.

Acute decompensation management: Emergency supportive care during metabolic crises includes intravenous glucose infusion to reverse catabolism, correction of metabolic acidosis, treatment of hyperammonemia, and intravenous L-carnitine. Prompt intervention prevents progression to encephalopathy and organ failure.

Newborn screening: NBS for PCD uses tandem mass spectrometry to measure free carnitine (C0) in dried blood spots. The condition is of high screening complexity due to pitfalls including maternal PCD, premature birth, and medication effects on C0 levels. Second-tier NGS testing can improve positive predictive value from approximately 5% to 20%.

Cardiac management: Standard heart failure therapy including ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, and diuretics may be required for patients presenting with cardiomyopathy and heart failure. Cardiac monitoring with echocardiography is essential for long-term follow-up. ICD placement may be considered for arrhythmia management.

Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling is essential for affected families, including discussion of autosomal recessive inheritance, 25% recurrence risk for each pregnancy, carrier testing for family members, and prenatal or preimplantation genetic testing options. NBS may also detect maternal PCD through low C0 in the infant.

Acetyl-L-carnitine for encephalopathy: Administration of acetyl-L-carnitine in patients with hepatic encephalopathy associated with carnitine deficiency can improve mental and psychological conditions, representing an adjunctive therapeutic approach.
Airway Clearance Techniques: Methods to help clear mucus from the lungs, such as chest physical therapy.
Antibiotic Therapy: Long-term or prophylactic antibiotics to control respiratory infections.
Bronchodilators: Medications to help open airways and ease breathing.
Nasal Steroids: Used to treat nasal polyps and chronic sinusitis.
Hearing Aids: Assistive devices for hearing loss management.
Genetic Counseling: Provides information and support for affected individuals and families.
Supportive Care: Symptomatic management and supportive interventions.

Ruxolitinib: JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor approved for intermediate-2 and high-risk PMF. Provides marked reduction in spleen size and constitutional symptoms but does not significantly alter disease course or prevent leukemic transformation.
Fedratinib: JAK2 inhibitor approved for intermediate-2 and high-risk PMF, including patients previously treated with ruxolitinib. Selective JAK2 inhibition with similar efficacy profile.

Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplantation: Only potentially curative treatment for PMF. Considered for younger patients with high-risk disease. Significant transplant-related morbidity and mortality must be weighed against disease risk.
Hydroxyurea: Cytoreductive therapy for symptomatic splenomegaly or thrombocytosis. Provides modest benefit but does not address constitutional symptoms as effectively as JAK inhibitors.
Anagrelide: Platelet-lowering agent used for thrombocytosis when present. Selective megakaryocyte inhibitor.
Speech Therapy and Counseling: Supportive management with speech therapy, counseling, and education for patients and caregivers.
Ursodeoxycholic Acid: May improve biochemistry but no proven survival benefit.
Endoscopic Therapy: Balloon dilation for dominant strictures.
Liver Transplantation: Only curative treatment; disease may recur.
Cholangiocarcinoma Surveillance: Regular imaging due to cancer risk.
Chemotherapy: Systemic treatment aimed at eradicating lymphoma cells, often using CHOP regimen (Cyclophosphamide, Doxorubicin, Vincristine, Prednisone).
Radiation Therapy: Targeted radiation to the tonsil and surrounding structures to control local disease.
Targeted Therapy: Use of drugs like rituximab for B-cell lymphomas.
Stem Cell Transplant: Considered in certain cases where high-dose chemotherapy is followed by infusion of healthy stem cells to restore bone marrow function.
Protein-restricted diet: Restriction of propiogenic amino acids (valine, isoleucine, threonine, methionine) with specialized formula support is the cornerstone of PA management.
Carnitine supplementation: Adjunctive therapy to support organic acid detoxification and excretion via formation of propionylcarnitine.

Carglumic acid (CGA): Carglumic acid is a synthetic structural analog of N-acetylglutamate (NAG) approved for treatment of hyperammonemia in PA. Chronic CGA use is associated with reduction in hyperammonemic decompensation episodes and hospital admissions.
Ammonia-lowering therapy: Sodium benzoate or sodium phenylbutyrate for pharmacologic ammonia reduction during hyperammonemic episodes.
Acute decompensation management: Emergency supportive care with reversal of catabolism via high glucose infusion, cessation of protein intake, and correction of metabolic acidosis.

Liver transplantation: Considered for severe recurrent decompensation and poor metabolic control. LT improves metabolic stability but does not fully correct the biochemical defect since PCC is expressed in extrahepatic tissues.

Cardiac pharmacotherapy: Standard cardiac therapies including ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, and coenzyme Q10 supplementation for management of cardiomyopathy and QTc prolongation. Multicenter approaches are needed to strengthen efficacy data.
Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling for affected families, including discussion of autosomal recessive inheritance, recurrence risk, and prenatal testing options.

Newborn screening: PA is detectable by newborn screening via tandem mass spectrometry using elevated C3-acylcarnitine as the primary marker. Early detection allows initiation of treatment before clinical decompensation.
AKT inhibitor (miransertib/ARQ 092): Allosteric pan-AKT inhibitor used to suppress AKT signaling and evaluated clinically for symptom and overgrowth control in Proteus syndrome.
Surgical intervention: Surgical debulking or corrective procedures used to manage focal overgrowth and deformity.
Topical Corticosteroids: First-line for limited disease.
Topical Vitamin D Analogues: Calcipotriene, calcitriol for mild to moderate disease.
Phototherapy: Narrowband UVB or PUVA for moderate to severe disease.
Methotrexate: Traditional systemic therapy for moderate to severe disease.
TNF Inhibitors: Adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab for moderate to severe disease.
IL-17 Inhibitors: Secukinumab, ixekizumab - highly effective targeted therapy.
IL-23 Inhibitors: Guselkumab, risankizumab - high efficacy with infrequent dosing.
IL-12/23 Inhibitor: Ustekinumab targets shared p40 subunit.
JAK Inhibitors: Oral therapy option (deucravacitinib - TYK2 inhibitor).
Targeted Cytokine Biologic Therapy: Biologic agents targeting IL-23/IL-17 and TNF pathways to reduce inflammation and control disease activity.
Phosphodiesterase-5 Inhibitors: Medications like sildenafil and tadalafil that relax blood vessels in the lungs
Endothelin Receptor Antagonists: Medications that block the effects of endothelin and help decrease blood pressure in the lungs
Prostacyclin Analogues: Medications that mimic the effects of prostacyclin, dilating pulmonary and systemic arterial vascular beds
Oxygen Therapy: Used to reduce symptoms in patients with hypoxemia
Anticoagulation: Used particularly in chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension to prevent clotting complications
Supportive Care: Fracture management and orthopedic surveillance. No targeted therapy available, though cathepsin K represents a potential therapeutic target.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for affected families given autosomal recessive inheritance.
Thyroidectomy: Total thyroidectomy with lymph node dissection is the primary treatment. Most RET fusion-positive PTCs have excellent outcomes with surgery alone.

Radioiodine Therapy: Adjuvant radioactive iodine therapy is used for intermediate and high-risk disease. RET fusion-positive tumors generally maintain radioiodine avidity better than BRAF-mutant tumors.
Selpercatinib: Highly selective RET inhibitor approved for RET fusion-positive thyroid cancer that is radioiodine-refractory. Demonstrates high response rates with durable disease control.
Pralsetinib: Another selective RET inhibitor with activity in RET fusion-positive thyroid cancer. Similar efficacy profile to selpercatinib.

Lenvatinib or Sorafenib: Multi-kinase inhibitors approved for radioiodine-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer. May be used but selective RET inhibitors are preferred for RET fusion-positive tumors.

Selpercatinib: Highly selective RET inhibitor approved for RET fusion-positive NSCLC based on LIBRETTO-001 trial. Excellent CNS penetration with demonstrated intracranial activity. Well-tolerated with manageable side effects (hypertension, LFT elevation).

Pralsetinib: Selective RET inhibitor approved for RET fusion-positive NSCLC based on ARROW trial. Active in treatment-naive and previously treated patients. Side effects include hypertension and pneumonitis.

Cabozantinib: Multi-kinase inhibitor (RET/VEGFR/MET) with RET activity. Less selective than selpercatinib/pralsetinib, used historically before selective inhibitors were available. More toxicity due to VEGFR inhibition.
Immunotherapy: Checkpoint inhibitors may be used for RET-positive NSCLC at progression, though driver mutation-positive tumors often have lower response rates to immunotherapy.
Chemotherapy: Platinum-based chemotherapy (pemetrexed-based) used at progression on RET inhibitors or historically before RET inhibitors were available.

Crizotinib: First ROS1 inhibitor approved for ROS1-rearranged NSCLC. Originally developed as ALK/MET inhibitor, cross-reactive with ROS1 due to kinase domain homology. Limited CNS penetration is a clinical limitation.
Entrectinib: ROS1/TRK/ALK inhibitor with excellent CNS penetration. Approved for ROS1-positive NSCLC including patients with CNS metastases. Also active against NTRK fusions.
Lorlatinib: Third-generation ALK/ROS1 inhibitor with activity against many resistance mutations including G2032R. Excellent CNS penetration. Used after progression on prior ROS1 inhibitors.
Repotrectinib: Next-generation ROS1/TRK inhibitor designed to overcome G2032R solvent front mutation. Compact macrocyclic structure allows binding despite steric hindrance from resistance mutations.
Chemotherapy: Platinum-based chemotherapy (pemetrexed-based) used at progression on ROS1 inhibitors or in combination with immunotherapy.

Beta-Blocker Therapy (Nadolol): Nonselective beta-blockers, particularly nadolol, are first-line therapy for CPVT. Nadolol is superior to beta1-selective agents in reducing exercise-induced ventricular arrhythmias. All patients with a clinical or genetic diagnosis of CPVT should receive beta-blocker therapy and avoid competitive sports and strenuous exercise.

Flecainide: Flecainide is used as add-on therapy in patients with breakthrough arrhythmias on beta-blockers. It directly inhibits RyR2 by open state block, reducing the mass of calcium sparks and preventing arrhythmogenic calcium waves.

Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator (ICD): ICD implantation is recommended for patients with inadequately controlled arrhythmias despite optimal pharmacotherapy, or survivors of cardiac arrest. ICD shocks can paradoxically trigger catecholamine surges and arrhythmia storms, so programming must be optimized.

Exercise Restriction: Avoidance of competitive sports and strenuous exercise is a cornerstone of CPVT management. Exercise provokes catecholamine release that triggers arrhythmias in susceptible individuals.
Rabies post-exposure vaccination: Post-exposure prophylaxis includes rabies vaccination.
Rabies immunoglobulin administration: Rabies immune globulin is used in post-exposure prophylaxis.

Calcium Channel Blockers: Dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (e.g., nifedipine, amlodipine) are first-line pharmacotherapy. They reduce frequency and severity of vasospastic attacks by inhibiting calcium influx into vascular smooth muscle cells.

Cold Avoidance and Protective Measures: Behavioral modification including wearing warm gloves, avoiding cold exposure, and using hand warmers. First-line non-pharmacological management for all patients with Raynaud phenomenon.

Phosphodiesterase-5 Inhibitors: Sildenafil and tadalafil improve digital blood flow by inhibiting PDE5, increasing cGMP levels, and enhancing nitric oxide-mediated vasodilation. Used as second-line therapy for refractory cases or when CCBs are insufficient.
Prostacyclin Analogues: Intravenous iloprost is used for severe Raynaud phenomenon with digital ischemia or ulceration. Provides potent vasodilation and antiplatelet effects.

Endothelin Receptor Antagonists: Bosentan blocks endothelin-1 receptor signaling and has shown effectiveness in treating and preventing digital ulcers, especially in patients with systemic sclerosis-associated Raynaud phenomenon with multiple ulcers.
Sympathectomy: Surgical sympathectomy can be used for severe, refractory Raynaud phenomenon to reduce sympathetic vasoconstriction. Long-term effectiveness is uncertain.
Controlled refeeding and caloric advancement: Gradual reintroduction of calories with careful monitoring to avoid rapid metabolic shifts.
Electrolyte supplementation and monitoring: Repletion of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium with close monitoring of serum electrolytes during refeeding.
Thiamine (vitamin B1) supplementation: Thiamine supplementation prior to and during refeeding to prevent deficiency and related complications.
Micronutrient supplementation during refeeding: Micronutrient supplementation during refeeding helps prevent deficiencies and refeeding syndrome.
Radical Nephrectomy: Surgical removal of the kidney for localized renal cell carcinoma.
Targeted Systemic Therapy: Molecularly targeted agents used to treat advanced renal cell carcinoma.
Belzutifan: HIF-2α inhibitor approved for treating renal cell carcinoma.
Focal Therapy (Laser/Cryotherapy): Small tumors may be treated with focal ablation including laser photocoagulation or cryotherapy. These treatments spare vision and avoid systemic toxicity.

Chemotherapy: Systemic chemotherapy with carboplatin, vincristine, and etoposide (CEV) reduces tumor size enabling focal consolidation. Intra-arterial chemotherapy delivers melphalan directly to the ophthalmic artery for localized effect.
Enucleation: Surgical removal of the eye is indicated for advanced intraocular disease, particularly when vision cannot be preserved. Provides excellent local control and prevents extraocular spread.

External Beam Radiation: Historically used for retinoblastoma but now avoided when possible due to significantly increased risk of secondary malignancies in RB1 germline mutation carriers, particularly osteosarcoma in the radiation field.

Botulinum Toxin Injection: Injection of botulinum toxin type A into the cricopharyngeus muscle to induce temporary relaxation. This is both diagnostic and therapeutic, with high success rates in restoring the ability to belch.
Seizure Management: Anticonvulsant medications for seizure control.
Physical Therapy: Maintain mobility, prevent contractures, manage scoliosis.
Occupational Therapy: Adaptive strategies for activities of daily living.
Speech Therapy: Augmentative and alternative communication strategies.
Nutritional Support: Address feeding difficulties and maintain adequate nutrition.
Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs (NSAIDs): Reduce pain and inflammation.
Disease-Modifying Antirheumatic Drugs (DMARDs): Slow disease progression and prevent joint damage (e.g., methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine).
Biologics: Target specific components of the immune system (e.g., TNF-α inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors).
Glucocorticoids: Potent anti-inflammatory agents used for short-term control of flares or as a bridge therapy.
Physical Therapy: Maintains joint mobility and strengthens surrounding muscles.
Immunoglobulin Replacement Therapy: Regular infusions to prevent infections.
Antibiotic Prophylaxis: Preventative antibiotic treatment to reduce infection risk.
Growth Hormone Therapy: May be used to address growth retardation.
Supportive Educational Programs: Tailored to address intellectual disability and developmental delays.
Surgical Interventions: For severe skeletal or craniofacial abnormalities.
Trigger avoidance and photoprotection: Baseline management includes avoidance of provoking exposures, gentle skin care, moisturization, and sun protection to reduce flares.

Papulopustular-directed pharmacotherapy: Papulopustular rosacea is commonly treated with topical metronidazole, azelaic acid, or ivermectin, and in more inflammatory disease with oral doxycycline or isotretinoin.
Topical brimonidine therapy: Persistent background erythema can be treated with topical brimonidine to reduce visible vasodilatory erythema.
Vascular light-based therapy: Persistent vascular erythema may also be treated with light-based therapy such as intense pulsed light.
Cyclosporine eye drops: Ocular rosacea may require targeted ophthalmic anti-inflammatory therapy such as cyclosporine eye drops.

Morbihan disease-directed pharmacotherapy: Rosacea-associated solid facial edema may require individualized combination pharmacotherapy centered on isotretinoin and intralesional corticosteroid treatment, with ketotifen used in selected refractory cases.
Laser surgical debulking for rhinophyma: CO2 laser or blue-laser debulking can remove phymatous nasal tissue and improve function and cosmesis in selected rhinophyma.
Excisional reconstruction for advanced rhinophyma: Severe rhinophyma may require excision with reconstructive approaches such as graft-based resurfacing when tissue overgrowth is extensive.
Growth hormone therapy: Growth hormone therapy for marked short stature in SADDAN.

Surgery and Chemotherapy-Centered Medulloblastoma Management: Treatment often emphasizes surgery and chemotherapy to limit radiotherapy exposure in high-risk childhood SUFU-associated medulloblastoma.

payload.disease: SUFU-related nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome

text: Genetic Counseling: Counseling and cascade testing are recommended because of inherited tumor risk.

payload.disease: SUFU-related nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome

text: Supportive Care: Management of symptoms and prevention of complications through multidisciplinary approach including physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and educational support. Treatment is primarily symptomatic as no causal therapies are currently available.

Base Editing Gene Therapy (Investigational): Emerging therapeutic approach using CRISPR-mediated adenine base editing to correct the common SLC17A5 c.115C>T (p.Arg39Cys) founder variant. In vitro and animal models show significant reduction in free sialic acid levels.
