Corticosteroid Therapy: First-line treatment for symptomatic sarcoidosis. Prednisone typically initiated at 20-40mg daily with gradual taper over months. Effective for most organ manifestations.
Methotrexate: Most commonly used steroid-sparing agent for chronic sarcoidosis. Allows reduction of corticosteroid dose while maintaining disease control.
Anti-TNF Therapy: Anti-TNF agents, particularly infliximab, are used for refractory sarcoidosis when corticosteroids and steroid-sparing agents are insufficient.

Corticosteroids: First-line treatment with the best overall results. 28 out of 30 cases responded to a regimen that included corticosteroids. Corticosteroid-containing regimens improved diarrhea in 6 out of 10 patients with GI involvement. None of the three patients who died had received corticosteroids or immunosuppressants.
Dantrolene: Muscle relaxant that improved muscle symptoms in 13 out of 15 cases, but did not improve any other symptoms of the disease. Used for symptomatic management of painful spasms.

Immunosuppressive Agents: Various immunosuppressants including cyclosporine, mycophenolate mofetil, azathioprine, methotrexate, tacrolimus, and cyclophosphamide have been used as steroid-sparing agents or to improve efficacy of treatment.
Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG): Used in some cases with variable response. Four out of nine patients treated with IVIG obtained a favorable response.
Permethrin cream or ivermectin: Mass treatment can use permethrin cream or ivermectin.
Praziquantel mass drug administration: Periodic praziquantel treatment to suppress morbidity in endemic regions.
Antipsychotic Medications: Medications such as risperidone, olanzapine, and clozapine to manage symptoms.
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT): Therapy aimed at changing maladaptive thought patterns and behaviors.
Social Skills Training: Programs to enhance social interactions and functioning.
Supportive Therapy: Ongoing support to help manage daily life and stress.
Vocational Rehabilitation: Help with gaining and maintaining employment.

Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling is recommended for families with an affected pregnancy given the autosomal recessive inheritance pattern and observed consanguinity in many reported families. Prenatal molecular testing is available when familial mutations are known.

Surgical repair: Surgical correction involves reimplantation or baffling of the anomalous pulmonary vein to the left atrium. Various techniques include direct reimplantation, creation of an intracardiac baffle, or newer multipatch techniques. Indicated for significant left-to-right shunting (Qp:Qs > 1.5-2.0), recurrent infections, or pulmonary hypertension. Postoperative pulmonary vein obstruction is a significant complication, occurring in up to 45-50% of cases with traditional techniques.

Catheter-based intervention: Transcatheter interventions include coil or plug embolization of anomalous systemic arteries (aortopulmonary collaterals) and occlusion of associated intracardiac defects. Catheter-based embolization of collateral arteries can reduce pulmonary overcirculation as a standalone procedure or as an adjunct to surgery.

Conservative management: Observation and medical management for asymptomatic patients with small shunts and no pulmonary hypertension. Regular follow-up with echocardiography to monitor for progressive right heart dilation or pulmonary hypertension. Medical management is effective for mild to moderate cases.
Surgical Decompression: Surgical decompression of cranial nerve foramina for progressive facial nerve palsy, hearing loss, or optic nerve compression.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for affected families, noting the high carrier frequency in the South African Afrikaner population.

Vitamin C Supplementation: Oral vitamin C supplementation (100-300 mg daily) rapidly reverses symptoms. Clinical improvement typically begins within 24-48 hours. The condition can be adequately treated and prevented via vitamin C supplementation.
Topical antifungal therapy: Topical antifungals are first-line therapy for mild-to-moderate scalp and facial disease to reduce Malassezia burden and inflammation.
Topical corticosteroid or calcineurin inhibitor therapy: Topical corticosteroids and calcineurin inhibitors are used to suppress active inflammation, especially in facial or body-fold disease.

payload.treatment_name: Topical corticosteroid or calcineurin inhibitor therapy

text: Topical roflumilast foam: Topical PDE4 inhibition offers a newer noncorticosteroid treatment option for seborrheic dermatitis.
Speech and Language Therapy: Communication support provided by specialist speech and language therapists.

Observation and Symptom Management: For mild cases, conservative management including avoidance of triggering stimuli and vestibular rehabilitation may be sufficient. Patients with pressure sensitivity can benefit from a tympanostomy tube.

Surgical Canal Plugging: Surgical occlusion of the dehiscent superior semicircular canal via middle cranial fossa or transmastoid approach to eliminate the third window effect. Canal plugging achieves long-term control more often than resurfacing.
Surgical Resurfacing: Surgical repair by placing bone graft or other material over the dehiscence to restore normal inner ear mechanics without occluding the canal lumen.
Coenzyme Q10 supplementation: Coenzyme Q10 used as part of mitochondrial supportive therapy.
Carnitine supplementation: L-carnitine provided as part of mitochondrial supportive therapy.
B vitamin supplementation: Vitamin B complex supplementation as part of supportive therapy.
ACE inhibitor therapy: ACE inhibitor therapy used for heart failure management.
Cardiac transplantation: Heart transplantation for severe cardiomyopathy in Sengers syndrome.
Diuretic therapy: Diuretics used for heart failure management in Sengers syndrome.
Levosimendan infusion: Levosimendan infusions used for heart failure support.
Antibiotic therapy: Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin) or azithromycin are first-line treatments for shigellosis. These antibiotics reduce duration of symptoms and bacterial shedding.
Oral rehydration therapy: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) with electrolytes to replace fluid and electrolyte losses.
Fluid and electrolyte replacement: Intravenous fluid replacement in severe cases with significant dehydration.
Supportive Care: SRPS are typically lethal in the perinatal period. Management is primarily supportive with palliative care.
Craniosynostosis Surgery: Surgical correction of craniosynostosis to prevent increased intracranial pressure and allow normal brain growth.
Orthopedic Management: Management of scoliosis, joint contractures, and other skeletal manifestations with bracing and surgery as needed.

Cardiovascular Monitoring: Regular echocardiographic surveillance for aortic root dilation and mitral valve prolapse. Vascular complications are less severe than in Loeys-Dietz or Marfan syndromes but require monitoring.
Hydroxyurea: Increases HbF, reduces pain crises, first-line disease-modifying therapy.
Voxelotor: HbS polymerization inhibitor, improves anemia.
Crizanlizumab: P-selectin inhibitor, reduces vaso-occlusive crises.
L-Glutamine: Reduces oxidative stress in RBCs.
Blood Transfusions: Chronic transfusions for stroke prevention, acute for severe anemia.
Pain Management: NSAIDs, opioids for acute crises.
Penicillin Prophylaxis: In children to prevent pneumococcal sepsis.
Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant: Curative option for selected patients.
Gene Therapy: Emerging curative approach (lovotibeglogene autotemcel).

Functional Endoscopic Sinus Surgery: Endoscopic uncinectomy and maxillary antrostomy to restore sinus ventilation and equalize pressure. This addresses the underlying cause by relieving ostial obstruction.

Orbital Floor Reconstruction: Surgical repair of the orbital floor using implants or grafts to correct enophthalmos when spontaneous resolution does not occur after sinus surgery. May be performed simultaneously with or staged after FESS.
Artificial Tears: Lubricating eye drops for symptomatic relief of dry eyes.
Pilocarpine: Muscarinic agonist to stimulate salivary secretion.
Hydroxychloroquine: For systemic manifestations including arthralgia and fatigue.
Rituximab: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody for severe systemic disease.
Dermatologic Care: Emollients, wound care for skin fragility, and monitoring for skin complications.
Cardiac Surveillance: Periodic echocardiographic screening is recommended given the potential for cardiac involvement to develop over time.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for autosomal recessive inheritance and carrier testing.

Platinum-Etoposide Chemotherapy: Carboplatin or cisplatin plus etoposide is the backbone of SCLC treatment. High initial response rates (60-80%) but nearly universal relapse. Four cycles is standard for extensive-stage; concurrent with radiation for limited-stage.

Atezolizumab (with Chemotherapy): PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitor added to carboplatin-etoposide for extensive-stage SCLC. IMpower133 trial showed modest overall survival benefit. Now standard first-line for extensive-stage.

Durvalumab (with Chemotherapy): PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitor added to platinum-etoposide for extensive-stage SCLC. CASPIAN trial showed overall survival benefit. Alternative to atezolizumab-based regimen.

Thoracic Radiation: Concurrent thoracic radiation with chemotherapy for limited-stage SCLC. Essential component of potentially curative therapy. Consolidative thoracic radiation may also benefit select extensive-stage patients.

Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation: Whole-brain radiation to prevent brain metastases in responding patients. Reduces brain metastasis incidence but controversial due to neurocognitive effects. Alternative is surveillance with MRI.
Lurbinectedin: Transcription inhibitor approved for relapsed SCLC. Alternative to topotecan for second-line treatment. Works by inhibiting oncogenic transcription factors.
Topotecan: Topoisomerase I inhibitor used for relapsed SCLC. Standard second-line option, though responses are generally modest and short-lived.
Antibiotic therapy: Rifaximin or other antibiotics to reduce bacterial overgrowth.
Dietary modification: Low-fermentable carbohydrate diets to reduce substrate for bacterial fermentation.
Prokinetic agents: Medications to improve intestinal motility and reduce stasis.
Antivenom administration: Intravenous antivenom is the only specific therapy to counteract envenoming.
Albendazole or mebendazole therapy: Benzimidazole anthelmintics used for treatment and preventive deworming.

Lower Body Negative Pressure: Application of negative pressure to the lower body to counteract cephalad fluid shift by drawing blood and fluids toward the legs. LBNP at 20 mmHg has been demonstrated to safely reduce intracranial pressure without impairing cerebral perfusion, and is a leading countermeasure candidate for SANS during spaceflight.

Mobile Lower Body Negative Pressure Suit: A wearable negative pressure suit that allows astronauts to apply caudal-directed fluid redistribution during normal crew activities, serving as a practical in-flight countermeasure.

payload.treatment_name: Mobile Lower Body Negative Pressure Suit

text: Nusinersen (Spinraza): Antisense oligonucleotide modifying SMN2 splicing to increase functional SMN protein, intrathecal administration.
Onasemnogene abeparvovec (Zolgensma): AAV9-based gene therapy delivering functional SMN1 gene, single IV infusion.
Risdiplam (Evrysdi): Oral small molecule SMN2 splicing modifier, daily oral administration.
Supportive Care: Respiratory support, nutritional management, physical therapy, orthopedic interventions.

Supportive and rehabilitative care: Multidisciplinary symptomatic management with physical therapy, speech therapy, mobility aids, swallow support, and psychiatric/cognitive care remains the standard of care.

Granulocyte-colony stimulating factor (preclinical): Preclinical disease-modifying candidate that improved neurologic function and reduced Purkinje cell pathology in transgenic SCA17 mice. This remains experimental and has not been established as human therapy.

Orthopedic Surveillance and Surgery: Management of progressive scoliosis and kyphosis (bracing, spinal fusion when severe), correction of limb deformities (osteotomy for coxa vara and genu valgum), hip arthroplasty for severe osteoarthritis, and monitoring for atlantoaxial instability with surgical stabilization if needed.

Ophthalmologic Surveillance: Regular ophthalmologic evaluation for myopia progression and retinal detachment screening. Type II collagen abnormalities in the vitreous create ongoing risk for retinal complications.
Audiologic Monitoring: Regular hearing assessments for early detection and management of sensorineural hearing loss.
Physical Therapy and Orthotic Devices: Rehabilitation and orthotic support to manage mobility limitations and prevent progressive deformity.
Cleft Palate Repair: Surgical repair when cleft palate is present.
Pulmonary Monitoring: Monitoring for restrictive lung disease secondary to severe kyphoscoliosis. Non-invasive ventilation support may be required when respiratory compromise develops.

Cervical Spine Stabilization: Evaluation and management of cervical instability from odontoid hypoplasia. Cervical spine imaging is essential before anesthesia, surgical procedures, or contact activities. Posterior cervical fusion may be required for significant atlantoaxial instability.

Orthopedic Management: Management of progressive hip dysplasia, kyphoscoliosis, limb deformities, and early-onset osteoarthritis. Procedures may include hip osteotomy, spinal fusion for progressive scoliosis, and eventual joint replacement for degenerative arthritis.

Ophthalmologic Surveillance: Regular eye examinations for myopia management and early detection of retinal detachment. Given the approximately 45% rate of myopia and 12% rate of retinal detachment, ongoing ophthalmologic follow-up is a standard of care.
Audiologic Monitoring: Regular hearing assessments given the 25-30% rate of sensorineural hearing loss. Hearing aids as needed for progressive impairment.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for affected individuals and families regarding autosomal dominant inheritance, variable expressivity, de novo mutation risk, and reproductive options.
Respiratory Support: Monitoring and management of respiratory compromise in severe neonatal cases, including ventilatory support and airway management for tracheobronchomalacia.

Growth Hormone Therapy: Growth hormone therapy has been evaluated in SEDC patients for short stature. In a small Chinese pediatric series, GH therapy resulted in modest height increases over 3-3.5 years with no significant side effects, though efficacy data remain limited and multi-center studies are needed.

Supportive Multidisciplinary Care: No disease-modifying therapy is available. Management is multidisciplinary and symptomatic, including physical therapy, pain management, orthopedic surveillance for kyphoscoliosis and cervical instability, and screening for odontoid hypoplasia before general anesthesia.
Low vision rehabilitation: Assistive devices, magnification aids, adaptive technologies, and rehabilitation strategies help maximize remaining functional vision and quality of life.
Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling is recommended for affected individuals and families to review inheritance, recurrence risk, and testing options.

Gene therapy (investigational): Several ABCA4 gene replacement strategies are under investigation. The large ABCA4 cDNA exceeds standard AAV packaging capacity, motivating dual-vector and lentiviral approaches.
Visual cycle modulators (investigational): Pharmacologic strategies such as emixustat and deuterated vitamin A derivatives aim to slow the visual cycle and reduce bisretinoid formation.

Stem cell therapy (investigational): Transplantation of retinal pigment epithelial cells derived from pluripotent stem cells is being explored to replace degenerated RPE and preserve retinal structure.

Lysosomal re-acidification (investigational): Drug-mediated lysosomal re-acidification is an RPE-targeted strategy intended to restore cathepsin D activity and improve photoreceptor outer segment degradation.
Complement inhibition (investigational): Complement pathway inhibitors are being explored to target inflammatory components of retinal pigment epithelium degeneration in Stargardt disease.

Immediate Drug Withdrawal: Prompt identification and cessation of the offending drug is the single most critical intervention in SJS management. Earlier withdrawal is associated with improved survival, as each additional day of exposure to the causative drug worsens outcomes.

Specialized Supportive Care: Management in burn centers or ICU settings with expertise in wound care, fluid and electrolyte replacement, nutritional support, temperature regulation, pain control, and infection prevention is associated with improved survival. Given the similarity to burn injuries, burn unit care is the cornerstone of management.

Cyclosporine: Cyclosporine A is the most effective immunosuppressive therapy for SJS, halting disease progression by suppressing CTL activity and blocking further keratinocyte killing. Multiple retrospective and prospective studies support its use in SJS, and it is considered first-line immunosuppressive treatment.

Intravenous Immunoglobulin: IVIG has been used to block Fas–FasL mediated apoptosis and modulate the immune response. Evidence for SJS-specific efficacy is mixed; combined IVIG with corticosteroids appears most effective for SJS/TEN overlap and TEN.

Systemic Corticosteroids: Systemic corticosteroids (e.g., dexamethasone, methylprednisolone) are among the most frequently used treatments for SJS/TEN despite ongoing controversy about benefit versus risk. They suppress immune activation and CTL-mediated keratinocyte killing but may increase infection risk in patients with open skin wounds. Short-course pulse regimens may reduce harm while capitalizing on anti-inflammatory efficacy.
Prophylactic Retinal Treatment: Prophylactic cryotherapy or laser photocoagulation to reduce retinal detachment risk. Regular ophthalmologic surveillance essential.
Hearing Management: Regular audiologic monitoring with hearing aids as needed for progressive hearing loss.
Joint Protection: Physical therapy, joint protection strategies, and management of early-onset osteoarthritis.
Cleft Repair: Surgical repair for cleft palate; management of Pierre Robin sequence if present.

Benzodiazepines: First-line symptomatic therapy. GABA-A receptor agonists that enhance GABAergic inhibition to reduce stiffness and spasms. Diazepam is most commonly used, starting at 5-10mg twice daily.
Baclofen: GABA-B receptor agonist used for spasticity control. Often used when benzodiazepines are insufficient or poorly tolerated. Can be given orally or intrathecally for refractory cases.

Intravenous Immunoglobulin: Immunomodulatory therapy that significantly reduces stiffness scores and improves mobility. Administered as 2g/kg divided over 2-5 days with monthly maintenance of 1g/kg. First-line immunotherapy effective in up to 75% of patients.

Rituximab: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that depletes B cells. Used as second-line immunotherapy when IVIg fails. Targets peripheral B-cell sources of autoantibodies. Dosing is empirical, typically 1-2g every 6-12 months.
Topical Keratolytics: Topical keratolytic agents (urea, salicylic acid) for symptomatic management of keratoderma.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for autosomal dominant inheritance with 50% recurrence risk.
Surgical Resection: Wide surgical resection with negative margins is the primary treatment for localized disease. Limb-sparing surgery is preferred when oncologically feasible.
Radiation Therapy: Adjuvant radiation improves local control, particularly for tumors larger than 5 cm, high grade, or with close/positive margins. May be given preoperatively or postoperatively.
Chemotherapy: Role of adjuvant chemotherapy is debated. Ifosfamide-based regimens are most commonly used in high-risk disease or metastatic setting. Doxorubicin is also active in synovial sarcoma.

Type I Interferon Receptor Antagonist: Anifrolumab targets the type I interferon receptor (IFNAR1) to block IFN signaling. Efficacy is enriched in patients with high IFN gene signatures, enabling mechanism-based patient stratification.

B Cell Depletion Therapy: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies (rituximab) and anti-CD19 CAR-T cell therapy target B cells, which are central to SLE pathogenesis through autoantibody production and antigen presentation.
BAFF Pathway Inhibition: Belimumab targets B cell activating factor (BAFF/TNFSF13B) to reduce B cell survival and activation, fitting the B cell tolerance failure paradigm.
Immunosuppressive Therapy: Corticosteroids, hydroxychloroquine, mycophenolate mofetil, azathioprine, and cyclophosphamide are used to suppress immune system activity and reduce inflammation.

Supportive Care: Management of specific organ manifestations including nephritis, neuropsychiatric symptoms, and cytopenias. Includes antihypertensives, anticoagulation, and renal replacement therapy as needed.
Calcium Channel Blockers: For Raynaud's phenomenon management.
Mycophenolate Mofetil: Immunosuppressant for skin and lung involvement.
Nintedanib: Antifibrotic agent for progressive interstitial lung disease.
ACE Inhibitors: Critical for scleroderma renal crisis management.
Cardiac surgery: Surgical repair of atrial septal defects and other congenital heart defects to correct hemodynamics and prevent complications
Palatal cleft repair: Surgical correction of cleft palate to restore normal feeding and speech development
Orthopedic surgery: Surgical management of talipes and other skeletal deformities
Airway management: Management of potential airway obstruction due to micrognathia and glossoptosis in Robin sequence
Multidisciplinary supportive care: Coordinated care from cardiology, orthopedics, otolaryngology, and genetics for comprehensive management

Corneal Transplantation: Penetrating keratoplasty or deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty for advanced disease with significant visual impairment. Recurrence of deposits in the graft is common, particularly for lattice and granular dystrophies.
Phototherapeutic Keratectomy: Excimer laser phototherapeutic keratectomy (PTK) can remove superficial deposits and improve visual acuity, particularly for Reis-Bucklers and Thiel-Behnke subtypes.

Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling regarding autosomal dominant inheritance with 50% recurrence risk. Genetic testing is recommended before refractive surgery (LASIK) as TGFBI mutations can cause severe corneal haze post-LASIK.
Multidisciplinary Care: A multidisciplinary team including genetics, dermatology, ophthalmology, otolaryngology, audiology, dentistry, plastic surgery, and nutrition is recommended.

Wound Care for Skin Erosions: Gentle wound care with dilute bleach soaks to prevent secondary infection. Aggressive monitoring for dehydration, electrolyte imbalances, and malnutrition in severe neonatal cases.
Cleft Lip/Palate Repair: Surgical repair of cleft lip and/or palate per routine protocols.
Dental Rehabilitation: Dentures in early childhood, dental implants in teens or early adulthood for hypodontia.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling regarding autosomal dominant inheritance with 70% de novo rate. Prenatal and preimplantation genetic testing available once the family variant is identified.
Antiparasitic therapy: Antiparasitic drugs are used to kill brain parasites in neurocysticercosis.
Surgical management: Surgical interventions may be used in selected cases.

Glucocorticoids: First-line treatment for active Takayasu arteritis. High-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) induce remission in the majority of patients, but relapse is common upon tapering.
Tocilizumab: Anti-IL-6 receptor monoclonal antibody used as steroid-sparing agent. In the TAKT phase 3 trial, tocilizumab showed a trend toward reduced relapse risk compared to placebo.

Vascular Surgery: Surgical revascularization or angioplasty for critical stenoses or aneurysms when disease is in remission. Bypass grafting is preferred over endovascular procedures due to lower restenosis rates.
Endodontic treatment with canal-focused planning: Endodontic therapy is feasible but requires careful canal identification and modified procedural strategy in taurodont teeth.
Supportive Care: Symptomatic management including seizure control, nutrition support, respiratory care.
Genetic Counseling: Carrier screening especially in high-risk populations.
Substrate Reduction Therapy: Investigational approaches to reduce GM2 synthesis.
Gene Therapy: Experimental AAV-based gene therapy in clinical trials.

Antiepileptic drugs: Standard antiepileptic medications are used to manage seizures in affected individuals, though specific drug efficacy may vary based on seizure type and individual response. Given the gain-of-function mechanism, channel blockade represents a rational therapeutic strategy, though precision anti-seizure efficacy for KCNH1 gain-of-function remains investigational.

Supportive developmental therapies: Comprehensive developmental support including physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy to maximize developmental potential despite severe intellectual disability.
Special education and behavioral support: Specialized educational interventions and behavioral support tailored to the severe intellectual disability and developmental needs of affected individuals.

Genetic counseling: Comprehensive genetic counseling is important for families given the autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, high de novo mutation rate, and documented cases of maternal mosaicism with variable penetrance.

Antiseizure Medication Therapy: Initial management of temporal lobe epilepsy is pharmacotherapy using antiseizure medications used across focal epilepsies, although many patients do not achieve durable seizure freedom and later meet criteria for drug-resistant disease.

Epilepsy Surgery for Drug-Resistant Disease: In carefully selected patients with drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy, surgical treatment offers substantially higher rates of seizure freedom than continued medical therapy and remains a key escalation path once pharmacoresistance is established.
Surgical Excision of Digital Fibromas: Recurrent digital fibromas may require surgical excision, though recurrence is common.
Genetic Counseling: Counseling regarding X-linked inheritance, male lethality, and variable expressivity due to X-inactivation patterns.

Radical Inguinal Orchiectomy: Radical inguinal orchiectomy is the primary diagnostic and therapeutic procedure for all suspected testicular tumors. It provides tissue for histological diagnosis and pathological staging. Trans-scrotal biopsy is contraindicated due to risk of tumor seeding.

BEP Chemotherapy: Bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin (BEP) is the standard first-line chemotherapy regimen for metastatic TGCT. Cisplatin-based chemotherapy cures over 80% of metastatic cases. Three cycles for good-risk and four cycles for intermediate/poor-risk disease.

Surveillance: Active surveillance is an option for stage I TGCT after orchiectomy, involving regular monitoring with tumor markers, imaging, and physical examination. In a prospective cohort, 15.1% of stage I patients relapsed on surveillance, all of whom were salvageable.

Radiation Therapy: Adjuvant radiation therapy to para-aortic lymph nodes is an option for stage I and IIA seminoma. It has largely been replaced by single-agent carboplatin or surveillance in stage I disease.

Retroperitoneal Lymph Node Dissection (RPLND): RPLND is used for staging and treatment of residual retroperitoneal masses after chemotherapy, particularly for non-seminomatous tumors with residual disease. It is also a primary treatment option for stage I NSGCT.
Tetanus Immunoglobulin (TIG): Human tetanus immunoglobulin provides passive immunity by neutralizing circulating toxin. Should be administered as early as possible.
Wound Debridement: Surgical cleaning and removal of necrotic tissue eliminates the anaerobic environment that supports C. tetani growth and toxin production.
Antibiotic Therapy: Metronidazole is the preferred antibiotic to eliminate C. tetani from the wound and prevent further toxin production. Penicillin is an alternative.
Muscle Relaxants and Sedation: Benzodiazepines (diazepam) are first-line for controlling spasms. Severe cases may require neuromuscular blocking agents and mechanical ventilation.
Supportive Care: ICU care including mechanical ventilation, nutritional support, and management of autonomic instability with beta-blockers or magnesium sulfate.
Active Immunization: Tetanus toxoid vaccine should be administered during recovery as natural infection does not confer immunity.
Exposure cessation and source removal: Management begins with immediate cessation of exposure and removal of the toxic source to prevent ongoing absorption.

Prussian blue therapy: Prussian blue is the preferred antidotal therapy for acute thallium poisoning because it enhances gastrointestinal elimination and has the best available safety profile among proposed drug therapies.
Multiple-dose activated charcoal: Single- or multiple-dose activated charcoal can be used to reduce ongoing gastrointestinal absorption and interrupt enteroenteric or enterohepatic recycling.
Potassium supplementation: Potassium supplementation has historically been used to enhance thallium elimination and remains part of classic treatment regimens.

Extracorporeal elimination adjuncts: Hemoperfusion or hemodialysis may be used as adjunctive therapy in severe poisoning, especially when neurologic toxicity is advanced or ongoing elimination support is needed.

Supportive and palliative care: Most affected infants die shortly after birth from respiratory failure. Palliative care is typically provided. Intensive respiratory support including tracheostomy and ventilation may prolong survival in rare cases, but long-term survival is exceptional. Management may also include shunt placement for hydrocephalus, suboccipital decompression, and anti-seizure medication.

FGFR3-targeted therapies (investigational): Preclinical approaches targeting FGFR3 signaling are under investigation for FGFR3-related skeletal dysplasias. In TD1, patient-derived iPSC chondrocytes treated with statins showed corrected cartilage formation. Related FGFR3-targeted strategies are also being studied in other FGFR3-driven skeletal dysplasias, but their application to TD1 remains investigational.

Supportive and palliative care: Most affected infants die shortly after birth from respiratory failure. Palliative care is typically provided. Rare long-term survivors require continuous ventilatory support, and some can be transitioned to home-based care with meticulous respiratory and nutritional management.

Statin therapy (preclinical): Statins (lovastatin, rosuvastatin) rescue FGFR3 skeletal dysplasia phenotypes in patient-derived iPSC models and ACH model mice, correcting degraded cartilage formation. This represents a potential pharmacological approach for FGFR3 chondrodysplasias, though clinical trials have not been conducted for TD2 given its typically perinatal lethality.
Antibiotic mass drug administration (SAFE strategy): Antibiotic mass drug administration reduces infection prevalence and transmission.
None Required: Condition is self-limiting and resolves without treatment.
Corticosteroid Therapy: High-dose steroids as standard acute therapy.
Plasma Exchange: Plasma exchange for more severe transverse myelitis.
Oral rehydration therapy: Fluid and electrolyte replacement is the cornerstone of treatment.
Loperamide: Antimotility agent for symptomatic relief in non-invasive cases.
Fluoroquinolone antibiotics: Empiric antibiotic therapy for moderate to severe cases.
Rifaximin: Nonabsorbable antibiotic option for traveler's diarrhea due to noninvasive E. coli.
Bismuth subsalicylate: Bismuth-based agent for symptom control and prophylaxis of traveler’s diarrhea.
Probiotic prophylaxis: Specific probiotic strains can reduce risk of traveler's diarrhea when taken before and during travel.
Education on safe food and water practices: Supportive counseling on safe food and water practices reduces risk and unnecessary antibiotic use during travel.

payload.treatment_name: Education on safe food and water practices

text: Rifaximin prophylaxis: Prophylactic rifaximin to reduce incidence of traveler’s diarrhea in high-risk itineraries with noninvasive E. coli exposure.

Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy: Standard treatment includes anthracycline and taxane-based regimens given before surgery. Pathologic complete response (pCR) rates are higher in TNBC than other subtypes and strongly predict long-term outcomes.

Pembrolizumab Plus Chemotherapy: PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab combined with chemotherapy improves outcomes in early-stage and metastatic TNBC. KEYNOTE-522 established pembrolizumab in the neoadjuvant/adjuvant setting regardless of PD-L1 status.
PARP Inhibitors: Olaparib and talazoparib are approved for germline BRCA-mutated metastatic TNBC. They exploit synthetic lethality in HRD-positive tumors unable to repair DNA double-strand breaks.
Sacituzumab Govitecan: Antibody-drug conjugate targeting Trop-2 with an SN-38 payload. Approved for previously treated metastatic TNBC with significant survival benefit demonstrated in ASCENT trial.
Platinum Chemotherapy: Carboplatin improves pCR rates in neoadjuvant therapy, particularly in BRCA-mutated or HRD-positive TNBC. Used in combination with taxanes.
Isoniazid: First-line antibiotic for TB treatment.
Rifampicin: Bactericidal antibiotic used for TB treatment.
Ethambutol: Bacteriostatic antibiotic used for TB treatment.
Directly Observed Therapy (DOT): Health worker observes and records patients taking their medication to ensure adherence.
Latent TB Treatment: Isoniazid or rifapentine used to prevent latent TB from becoming active.
Host-Directed Therapy: Adjunctive strategies to enhance autophagy, modulate inflammasome activity, or reprogram macrophage lipid metabolism alongside standard antibiotics.

mTOR Inhibitor Therapy (Everolimus): Everolimus is an mTOR inhibitor with FDA approval for TSC-associated subependymal giant cell astrocytomas, renal angiomyolipomas, and as adjunctive treatment for refractory seizures.
Vigabatrin for Infantile Spasms: Vigabatrin is the first-line treatment for TSC-associated infantile spasms, with high response rates specific to this population.
Epilepsy Surgery: Surgical resection may be considered for drug-resistant seizures when a single epileptogenic tuber can be identified.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling is recommended for TSC families given the autosomal dominant inheritance pattern and variable expressivity.

Fetoscopic laser photocoagulation: The standard of care for TTTS Quintero Stage II and above. A fetoscope is introduced into the recipient sac under ultrasound guidance and a laser is used to coagulate all placental vascular anastomoses connecting the two fetal circulations. The Solomon technique (dichorionization) involves coagulating the entire vascular equator to reduce the risk of recurrent TTTS and twin anemia-polycythemia sequence (TAPS). High-volume centers report up to 70% double survival and at least one survivor in over 90% of cases.

Amnioreduction: Serial drainage of excess amniotic fluid from the recipient sac to reduce uterine overdistension, relieve maternal symptoms, and decrease the risk of preterm labor. Considered a palliative approach and may be used when laser therapy is not available or for Stage I disease.

Supportive neonatal care: Both donor and recipient twins require specialized neonatal care after delivery, which is often premature. The donor may need transfusion for anemia and management of growth restriction. The recipient may require treatment for cardiac dysfunction, polycythemia, and hyperbilirubinemia.

Intrauterine fetal transfusion: In cases complicated by severe fetal anemia (such as twin anemia-polycythemia sequence following laser therapy), intrauterine transfusion may be performed to correct the anemia.
Metformin: First-line oral medication that reduces hepatic glucose production and improves insulin sensitivity.
Lifestyle Modification: Diet and exercise interventions to reduce weight and improve metabolic health.
GLP-1 Receptor Agonists: Injectable medications that enhance insulin secretion and promote weight loss.
SGLT2 Inhibitors: Oral medications that increase urinary glucose excretion.
Insulin Therapy: Required when beta cell function declines significantly.
GLP-1/GIP Dual Agonists: Tirzepatide and similar agents that activate both GLP-1 and GIP receptors for enhanced glycemic control and weight loss.
Insulin Therapy: Essential treatment to replace the missing insulin and regulate blood glucose levels.
Blood Glucose Monitoring: Regular monitoring to manage glucose levels effectively.
Lifestyle Modifications: Maintaining a balanced diet, regular physical activity, and adherence to treatment plans.
Immunotherapy (Teplizumab): T-cell-targeting therapy that delays progression from stage 2 to stage 3 disease by approximately 2-3 years in early-stage patients.
Continuous Glucose Monitoring: Real-time glucose monitoring systems for improved glycemic control and reduced hypoglycemic events.

Nitisinone (NTBC) therapy: Nitisinone (2-[2-nitro-4-trifluoromethylbenzoyl]-1,3-cyclohexanedione) is a potent inhibitor of 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase (HPD), the second enzyme in tyrosine degradation. By blocking HPD, NTBC prevents formation of the toxic downstream metabolites FAA, MAA, and SA. Early initiation of NTBC, ideally before symptom onset via NBS identification, provides greater than 90% survival and dramatically reduces the need for liver transplantation.

Dietary tyrosine and phenylalanine restriction: Low-tyrosine, low-phenylalanine diet is essential in combination with NTBC therapy, since NTBC causes upstream accumulation of tyrosine. Specialized medical formulas provide essential amino acids while restricting tyrosine and phenylalanine intake.

Liver transplantation: Liver transplantation is curative for the hepatic manifestations of HT-1 but does not correct renal FAH deficiency, and residual SA production by the kidneys may persist. Indications include acute liver failure, HCC or dysplastic nodules, or failure of medical management.

Newborn screening: NBS for HT-1 using dried blood spot succinylacetone measurement is recommended, as it is superior to tyrosine-based screening. Early identification enables pre-symptomatic NTBC initiation for optimal long-term outcomes.

Supportive care: Acute and chronic supportive management includes correction of coagulopathy, electrolyte management for renal tubular dysfunction, vitamin D and phosphate supplementation for rickets, and monitoring of liver and renal function.

Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling for affected families, including discussion of autosomal recessive inheritance, 25% recurrence risk, carrier testing, and prenatal or preimplantation genetic diagnosis options.

Neurocognitive assessment and therapy: Long-term neurocognitive assessment is recommended for HT-1 patients, as they are at risk for neurocognitive impairment even under NTBC therapy. Early intervention and educational support may improve outcomes.
5-Aminosalicylates: First-line for mild-moderate disease (mesalamine).
Corticosteroids: For acute flares (prednisone, budesonide).
Thiopurines: Azathioprine, 6-MP for maintenance.
Anti-TNF Therapy: Infliximab, adalimumab for moderate-severe disease.
Vedolizumab: Gut-selective integrin inhibitor.
Tofacitinib: JAK inhibitor for moderate-severe disease.
Ustekinumab: IL-12/23 inhibitor.
Colectomy: Curative surgery for refractory or complicated disease.
Surgical Reconstruction: Surgical correction of upper limb deformities and breast reconstruction as needed, depending on severity of malformations.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for affected families given autosomal dominant inheritance pattern and variable expressivity.

Protein-restricted diet: Controlled protein intake to reduce nitrogen load while preserving growth is the cornerstone of chronic UCD management. Natural protein is limited and supplemented with essential amino acid mixtures to maintain adequate nutrition and growth.

Nitrogen scavenger therapy: Sodium phenylbutyrate is metabolized to phenylacetate, which conjugates with glutamine to form phenylacetylglutamine, providing an alternative renal route for waste nitrogen excretion. Glycerol phenylbutyrate is a newer palatable formulation. Sodium benzoate conjugates with glycine to form hippurate for renal excretion.

Arginine and citrulline supplementation: Arginine supplementation replaces the amino acid that cannot be synthesized endogenously in most UCDs (except ARG1 deficiency). Citrulline supplementation may be preferable as it bypasses intestinal and hepatic first-pass metabolism and has been associated with lower mean ammonia concentrations compared to arginine alone.

Carglumic acid: Carglumic acid (Carbaglu) is a synthetic analog of N-acetylglutamate that directly activates CPS1. It is specifically indicated for NAGS deficiency and is also used for hyperammonemia in organic acidemias. It provides pharmacological bypass of the NAGS enzyme deficiency.

Acute hyperammonemia management: Emergency management includes cessation of protein intake, high-calorie glucose infusion to reverse catabolism, intravenous nitrogen scavengers (sodium benzoate, sodium phenylacetate), and arginine supplementation. When ammonia exceeds 500 umol/L or encephalopathy is present, extracorporeal clearance with continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) is recommended.

Liver transplantation: Liver transplantation remains the only curative option for severe UCDs, restoring full urea cycle capacity. It is indicated for patients with recurrent severe hyperammonemic crises despite maximal medical therapy. Benefits include elimination of hyperammonemia risk, dietary liberalization, and improved quality of life, but carry lifelong immunosuppression requirements.

Newborn screening: Some UCDs are detectable through expanded newborn screening by tandem mass spectrometry. Citrullinemia type I (elevated citrulline) and ASL deficiency (elevated argininosuccinic acid) are most reliably detected. Early identification enables pre-symptomatic treatment initiation and prevention of neonatal crises.

Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling for affected families, including discussion of inheritance patterns (autosomal recessive for most UCDs, X-linked for OTC deficiency), recurrence risk assessment, carrier testing, and prenatal or preimplantation genetic diagnosis options.

Tebentafusp: Bispecific T-cell engager (TCE) that binds gp100 peptide presented by HLA-A*02:01 on tumor cells and CD3 on T cells, redirecting T cells to kill melanoma cells. First systemic therapy to improve overall survival in metastatic uveal melanoma. Requires HLA-A*02:01 positive status (approximately 50% of patients).

Plaque Brachytherapy: Radiation therapy using radioactive plaque sutured to the sclera overlying the tumor. First-line local therapy for many uveal melanomas, achieving excellent local control while preserving the eye in most cases.

Enucleation: Surgical removal of the eye. Indicated for large tumors, those with optic nerve involvement, or when vision cannot be preserved. Provides excellent local control but does not prevent metastatic disease.

Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors: Anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4 antibodies have limited efficacy in uveal melanoma compared to cutaneous melanoma, with response rates of approximately 5-10%. The immunologically cold tumor microenvironment may contribute to this resistance.

Avoidance of fasting: Frequent feeding and fasting limitation to prevent catabolic stress is the cornerstone of VLCAD management. Maximum fasting duration is age-dependent and shorter than in healthy individuals.

Medium-chain triglyceride supplementation: MCT supplementation provides an alternative energy substrate that bypasses the VLCAD enzyme block. In long-chain FAODs, MCT is supplemented at 10-25% of total energy, with total MCT plus LCT maintained at 20-35% of energy.

Long-chain fat restriction: Dietary restriction of long-chain triglycerides to approximately 10% of total energy intake, while ensuring adequate essential fatty acid supply and monitoring fat-soluble vitamins.

Triheptanoin (UX007): FDA-approved odd-chain triglyceride therapy for long-chain FAODs. Triheptanoin provides anaplerotic substrates (propionyl-CoA) that support the TCA cycle and gluconeogenesis, dosed at approximately 20-35% of total energy intake. GI adverse effects may improve with dose fractionation.
Emergency glucose therapy: Rapid carbohydrate support during illness or decompensation via intravenous glucose (at least 10% dextrose) to reverse catabolism and prevent further lipolysis.

Pre-exercise carbohydrate or MCT loading: Patients at risk of rhabdomyolysis should ingest MCT or carbohydrates or a combination of both approximately 20 minutes before exercise to provide alternative energy substrates and reduce reliance on impaired long-chain FAO.
Supportive care for cardiomyopathy: Standard cardiac management including pharmacotherapy for heart failure and arrhythmia monitoring for patients with cardiac involvement.

Newborn screening: VLCAD deficiency is detectable by newborn screening via tandem mass spectrometry using C14:1 acylcarnitine as the primary marker, followed by confirmatory plasma/serum acylcarnitines, lymphocyte VLCAD enzyme activity assay, and ACADVL molecular testing.
Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling for affected families, including discussion of autosomal recessive inheritance, recurrence risk, carrier testing, and prenatal diagnostic options.
Surgical Decompression: Surgical decompression of cranial nerve foramina for progressive cranial nerve compression symptoms.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for affected families given autosomal recessive inheritance.
Lamotrigine: Anticonvulsant that may reduce symptoms in some patients by reducing cortical hyperexcitability. Provides partial relief in approximately 20% of patients.
Tinted Lenses: FL-41 tinted lenses and chromatic filters may provide symptom relief in some patients.
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy: Psychological intervention that may help patients cope with symptoms and improve quality of life.
Benzodiazepines: May provide symptom relief in some patients, though long-term use carries risks.
Topical Corticosteroid: Common first-line treatment to reduce inflammation and potentially stimulate repigmentation.

JAK Inhibitor Therapy: Targeting the IFN-gamma-JAK/STAT signaling pathway with JAK inhibitors (e.g., topical ruxolitinib) represents a leading therapeutic strategy. Often combined with narrowband UVB phototherapy to enhance repigmentation efficacy.
Phototherapy: UVB light or PUVA treatment can stimulate melanocyte regeneration and pigment production.
Skin Graft: can be used for stable vitiligo.
Melanocyte Transplantation: can be used for stable vitiligo.
Monobenzone Cream: Treatment with monobenzone cream to remove remaining pigment in cases of extensive vitiligo.

Surveillance Protocol: Comprehensive surveillance beginning in childhood includes annual ophthalmologic examination (from age 1), annual plasma metanephrines (from age 5), annual abdominal ultrasound (from age 8), and MRI brain/spine every 2 years (from age 11). Earlier imaging if symptoms occur.

Nephron-Sparing Surgery: Partial nephrectomy is the preferred approach for renal tumors to preserve renal function given the high likelihood of bilateral/multifocal disease. Surgery is typically recommended when tumors reach 3 cm to balance cancer risk with nephron preservation.

Belzutifan: HIF-2alpha inhibitor FDA-approved for VHL-associated RCC, hemangioblastomas, and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors not requiring immediate surgery. First targeted therapy directly addressing the molecular defect in VHL disease. Objective response rates of 49% in RCC and 30-77% across tumor types.

Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling and testing for at-risk family members. Children of affected parents have 50% risk. Predictive testing allows targeted surveillance in mutation carriers and can reassure non-carriers. Prenatal and preimplantation genetic testing are options.
Ceftriaxone induction followed by trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole: Two-week intravenous ceftriaxone induction followed by oral TMP-SMX maintenance to eradicate infection and prevent relapse.
Doxycycline plus hydroxychloroquine regimen: Oral doxycycline and hydroxychloroquine for one year, followed by lifelong doxycycline for relapse prevention.

Surgical Resection: Nephrectomy is the primary treatment for unilateral Wilms tumor. In North America (COG), upfront nephrectomy provides staging information and allows for immediate histologic diagnosis and molecular analysis. In Europe (SIOP), preoperative chemotherapy is given first. Both approaches achieve similar survival rates.

Chemotherapy: Actinomycin D and vincristine form the backbone of treatment for favorable histology tumors. Doxorubicin and other agents are added for higher-stage or anaplastic tumors. Cyclophosphamide and etoposide are added for the highest-risk patients.

Radiation Therapy: Flank radiation is used for stage III tumors and higher. Whole abdominal radiation is used for tumor rupture or diffuse peritoneal contamination. Whole lung irradiation is used for pulmonary metastases that fail to respond completely to chemotherapy.
D-Penicillamine: Copper chelator, first-line therapy, promotes urinary copper excretion.
Trientine: Alternative copper chelator, better tolerated than penicillamine.
Zinc Acetate: Blocks intestinal copper absorption, maintenance therapy.
Low Copper Diet: Avoid liver, shellfish, chocolate, nuts, mushrooms.
Tetrathiomolybdate: Emerging copper chelator that rapidly complexes free copper, reducing bioavailable copper.
Liver Transplantation: Curative for hepatic Wilson disease, reverses copper metabolism defect.
Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs: NSAIDs can reduce fever and inflammatory symptoms.
Corticosteroids: Systemic corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone) used as part of combination regimens to control inflammation.
Dapsone: Dapsone was used alongside NSAIDs and corticosteroids in a reported case.
Azathioprine: Immunosuppressive therapy used in combination with corticosteroids.
Seizure Management with Levetiracetam: Levetiracetam has shown efficacy in controlling seizures in Wolf-Hirschhorn syndrome patients.
Seizure Management with Valproic Acid: Valproic acid is commonly used for epilepsy management in Wolf-Hirschhorn syndrome.
Growth Hormone Therapy: Recombinant human growth hormone therapy for patients with concurrent growth hormone deficiency.
Early Aggressive Seizure Management: Early aggressive management using multiple antiseizure medications may be critical to improve neurological prognosis.

Burosumab (Crysvita): Anti-FGF23 monoclonal antibody. Directly targets the pathogenic mechanism by neutralizing excess FGF23, normalizing phosphate homeostasis. FDA approved 2018 for pediatric and adult XLH. Administered subcutaneously every 2-4 weeks.

Conventional Therapy (Phosphate/Calcitriol): Oral phosphate supplementation combined with active vitamin D (calcitriol or alfacalcidol). Previously standard of care but limited by GI side effects, secondary hyperparathyroidism, and nephrocalcinosis risk.
Orthopedic Management: Corrective osteotomies for significant lower limb deformity. Timing typically after optimization of medical therapy and near completion of growth.
Azithromycin therapy: Oral azithromycin is preferred for treatment.
Supportive care: Multidisciplinary management including physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and management of seizures when present.
Cataract surgery: Surgical removal of congenital cataracts can improve visual responses and social interactions.
Supportive care: Multidisciplinary supportive care is central, especially for developmental and behavioral management.
Antiseizure medication: Antiseizure medications are used for seizure control, although response is variable and seizures may be refractory.
Supportive care: Multidisciplinary supportive management is the mainstay and includes developmental services and specialty surveillance.
Speech therapy: Speech therapy is often needed for language delay and communication difficulties.
Physical therapy: Physical therapy can address hypotonia, motor delay, and coordination issues.

Valine-Restricted Diet: Dietary restriction of valine is the primary targeted therapy for HIBCH deficiency, addressing the block in valine catabolism. Special medical food formulas can be delivered orally or by gastrostomy tube, with total protein restriction as an alternative when formula adherence is poor.

Supportive metabolic and dietary management: HIBCH deficiency has no established curative therapy, but reported patients have received drug and dietary management with clinical improvement in some cases.
Hormone Replacement Therapy: Estrogen-based hormone replacement is used to induce secondary sexual characteristics and reduce osteoporosis risk in hypoestrogenic 46,XX gonadal dysgenesis.
Prophylactic gonadectomy: Early gonadectomy is used to reduce the substantial gonadal malignancy risk associated with dysgenetic streak gonads.

Transsphenoidal surgery and multimodal local control: Large AIP-associated somatotropinomas are usually treated with transsphenoidal surgery, with adjuvant medical therapy and radiotherapy considered for residual or invasive disease.

payload.treatment_name: Transsphenoidal surgery and multimodal local control

text: Reduced effectiveness of first-generation somatostatin analogs: AIP-mutant somatotroph tumors frequently respond less well to first-generation somatostatin receptor ligands such as octreotide or lanreotide, often necessitating alternative or combination therapy.

payload.treatment_name: Reduced effectiveness of first-generation somatostatin analogs

text: Dopamine agonist and pegvisomant escalation for resistant disease: Mixed GH/PRL tumors or resistant somatotropinomas may require dopamine agonists and GH receptor antagonist therapy as part of multimodal disease control.

payload.treatment_name: Dopamine agonist and pegvisomant escalation for resistant disease

text: Radiotherapy for residual or recurrent tumor control: Radiotherapy remains an accepted adjunct for residual, recurrent, or inoperable AIP-associated pituitary tumors when surgery and medical therapy are insufficient.

payload.treatment_name: Radiotherapy for residual or recurrent tumor control

text: Gene Augmentation Therapy (CNGA3): Subretinal delivery of AAV8 vectors carrying wild-type CNGA3 cDNA under a cone-specific promoter (human cone arrestin ARR3 promoter). Phase I clinical trial (NCT02610582) demonstrated safety and preliminary evidence of improved cone function including visual acuity gains and improved contrast sensitivity at 1-year and sustained at 3-year follow-up.

Gene Augmentation Therapy (CNGB3): Subretinal AAV5-mediated delivery of CNGB3 to restore cone CNG channel function. Preclinical studies in CNGB3-mutant dogs demonstrated stable restoration of cone function for at least 33 months. Clinical trials (NCT02599922, NCT03001310) are evaluating safety and efficacy in CNGB3-achromatopsia patients.

Tinted Contact Lenses and Filters: Red or magenta-tinted contact lenses or filter glasses reduce photophobia and may improve visual comfort and contrast sensitivity by reducing rod saturation in photopic conditions.
Low Vision Aids: Magnification devices, high-contrast displays, and adaptive technologies help patients manage reduced visual acuity in daily activities.
Recombinant human growth hormone therapy: Recombinant human growth hormone has been used to improve height outcomes in selected patients with acrodysostosis.
Immunoglobulin replacement therapy: Intravenous or subcutaneous immunoglobulin replacement is standard supportive therapy for antibody deficiency and reduces infectious burden.
Sirolimus: mTOR inhibition with sirolimus is used as pathway-directed therapy for benign lymphoproliferation and selected immune-dysregulation manifestations.

Leniolisib: Selective oral PI3Kdelta inhibition directly targets the causal signaling lesion in APDS and improves lymphadenopathy, abnormal B-cell subsets, serum IgM, and broader immune dysregulation.

Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation: Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation remains the only potentially curative option and is reserved for severe, complicated, or treatment-refractory disease.
Intravenous hemin: Standard treatment for severe acute attacks; exogenous hemin represses hepatic ALAS1 and is more effective than glucose alone in serious attacks.
Givosiran: Liver-directed siRNA prophylaxis that lowers ALAS1, reduces ALA and PBG, and markedly decreases recurrent attack burden.
Carbohydrate loading: High-carbohydrate intake is used as adjunctive therapy during milder attacks but is usually insufficient alone for serious episodes.

Wound Care for Aplasia Cutis: Conservative wound management of scalp defects with moist wound dressings and infection prevention. Most small to moderate aplasia cutis lesions heal spontaneously with conservative care.

Surgical Reconstruction: Surgical intervention for large scalp defects, exposed dura, or significant limb deficiencies. May include skin grafting, tissue expansion, or prosthetic fitting for limb defects.

Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for families with AOS to discuss inheritance patterns, recurrence risks, and available genetic testing options. Important given the genetic heterogeneity (6 known genes) and variable inheritance patterns (autosomal dominant and recessive forms).
Methionine-Restricted Diet: Dietary methionine intake restriction has been tried, but outcomes are variable and it is not curative.
Liver transplantation: Liver transplantation can correct the hepatic component, although neurologic disease may continue to progress.

Antiepileptic Drug Therapy: Seizure management with antiepileptic drugs including ACTH and vigabatrin for initial infantile spasms, followed by levetiracetam, clobazam, valproic acid, and others. Seizures are nearly universally refractory to medication.

Ketogenic Diet Therapy: High-fat, low-carbohydrate therapeutic diet. In a study of 15 patients, 67% experienced at least 50% seizure reduction at 3 months, and 20% achieved at least 90% reduction. Seizure freedom was rare. More effective in patients without infantile spasms at initiation.

Epilepsy Surgery: Surgical options for selected cases include hemispherectomy, cortical resection, and corpus callosotomy. Most patients do not qualify due to multiple areas of epileptogenesis. Outcomes are variable.
Scoliosis Surgery: Orthopedic surgical intervention for progressive scoliosis. Bracing is ineffective in Aicardi syndrome, necessitating early surgical referral.
Supportive Rehabilitation: Multidisciplinary rehabilitation including physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy to maximize developmental potential.
Genetic Counseling: Recurrence risk counseling for families. Recurrence risk for siblings is believed to be less than 1% due to the de novo nature of mutations.
Maralixibat: Ileal bile acid transporter inhibition with maralixibat is used to reduce cholestatic pruritus in Alagille syndrome.
Liver transplantation: Liver transplantation is used for severe hepatic disease, growth failure, portal hypertension, or refractory pruritus.

ACE Inhibitor Therapy: Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (e.g., ramipril, enalapril) are the cornerstone of treatment, shown to delay progression to ESRD by reducing proteinuria and glomerular hyperfiltration. Early initiation at diagnosis is now recommended for X-linked males and autosomal recessive patients.

Angiotensin Receptor Blocker Therapy: ARBs are used as an alternative or addition to ACE inhibitors for patients who cannot tolerate ACE inhibitors. RAAS blockade is the foundation of treatment for Alport syndrome.

SGLT2 Inhibitor Therapy: Sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors may offer additional kidney protection as adjunctive therapy to RAAS blockade in adults with proteinuria and CKD. A pediatric RCT (DOUBLE PRO-TECT Alport, NCT05944016) is underway.
Renal Replacement Therapy: Dialysis (hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis) is required when patients progress to end-stage renal disease. This serves as a bridge to kidney transplantation.

Kidney Transplantation: Kidney transplantation is the definitive treatment for ESRD in Alport syndrome. Outcomes are generally excellent, though a small percentage of transplanted patients develop anti-GBM nephritis due to immune response against the novel alpha3-alpha4-alpha5(IV) collagen in the allograft.
GLP-1 Receptor Agonist Therapy: Semaglutide or exenatide can improve weight, glycemia, and lipid parameters in adults with Alstrom syndrome and metabolically significant obesity or diabetes.
Hearing aid usage: Sound amplification is beneficial for the common progressive sensory hearing loss of Alstrom syndrome.
Cochlear implantation: Cochlear implantation should be considered for severe or functionally limiting hearing loss not adequately managed with amplification.
Flunarizine prophylaxis: Flunarizine is the most commonly used preventive medication for reducing the frequency and intensity of paroxysmal events in AHC.
Supportive trigger avoidance and sleep induction: Trigger avoidance and rapid sleep induction are practical nonpharmacologic measures used to abort or reduce attacks.

payload.treatment_name: Supportive trigger avoidance and sleep induction

text: Antiseizure therapy: Anti-seizure medication is used when epilepsy is present, although seizures are frequently drug resistant and may require escalation.
Anthracycline-Based Combination Chemotherapy: Anthracycline-containing multiagent chemotherapy remains a core systemic treatment backbone for ALK-positive systemic ALCL.
Brentuximab Vedotin Plus CHP: Frontline brentuximab vedotin plus CHP improved progression-free and overall survival over CHOP in previously untreated systemic ALCL and other CD30-positive PTCL.
Single-Agent Brentuximab Vedotin: In relapsed or refractory systemic ALCL, single-agent brentuximab vedotin can produce durable complete remissions in a subset of patients.
Crizotinib: ALK inhibition with crizotinib has meaningful activity in relapsed or refractory systemic ALK-positive ALCL.
Radiation Therapy: Localized primary cutaneous ALCL can be treated with skin-directed radiation therapy.
Complete Surgical Excision with Implant Removal: Breast implant-associated ALCL is primarily managed with complete surgical excision, including total capsulectomy and implant removal.

payload.treatment_name: Complete Surgical Excision with Implant Removal

text: Surgery and Airway Stabilization: Selected localized tumors may undergo aggressive resection, but airway preservation or tracheostomy is often the more urgent intervention because many patients present with compressive neck disease.
External Beam Radiation Therapy: External beam radiation is used for locoregional control, palliation of compressive symptoms, and as part of multimodality treatment when feasible.
Dabrafenib Plus Trametinib: Combined BRAF and MEK inhibition provides substantial clinical benefit in BRAF V600E-mutant ATC and is a core systemic option for molecularly selected patients.
Surgical Excision: Surgery remains the standard local treatment for resectable angiosarcoma and is often combined with other modalities because of high recurrence risk.
Adjuvant Radiation Therapy: Radiation therapy is commonly used with surgery in localized cutaneous angiosarcoma, although recurrence remains frequent.
Paclitaxel-Based Chemotherapy: Taxane-based chemotherapy is a standard systemic option for advanced angiosarcoma and is commonly used in cutaneous and advanced disease.
Anthracycline-Based Chemotherapy: Anthracycline-based chemotherapy is another main systemic treatment option for advanced angiosarcoma.

Anti-Angiogenic Therapy: Anti-angiogenic agents are used in advanced angiosarcoma, reflecting the central role of endothelial growth factor signaling in disease biology, although responses are often not durable.
Immune Checkpoint Inhibition: Checkpoint blockade appears most promising in ultraviolet-associated cutaneous angiosarcoma with higher tumor mutation burden and immune-active microenvironments.
Cognitive behavioral therapy: Cognitive behavioral therapy is a core psychotherapy for anorexia nervosa and is included among treatments of choice.
Family-based therapy: Family-based therapy is a treatment of choice for children and adolescents with anorexia nervosa.
Olanzapine pharmacotherapy for weight gain: Olanzapine has moderate evidence for supporting weight gain in anorexia nervosa, while psychopharmacotherapy otherwise lacks clear efficacy.
Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement: ICD implantation is used to prevent sudden cardiac death in symptomatic or otherwise high-risk patients.
Antiarrhythmic pharmacotherapy: Antiarrhythmic drugs are used for symptomatic arrhythmia control.
Catheter ablation: Catheter ablation is used for recurrent ventricular tachycardia in selected patients.
S-Adenosylmethionine Supplementation: SAM supplementation is the best-described reported intervention for Arts syndrome and has been associated with stabilization or improvement.

Nicotinamide Riboside Supplementation: Nicotinamide riboside has been reported in combination with SAMe in Arts syndrome and is mechanistically relevant because PRPS1 deficiency affects NAD-related metabolism.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling is appropriate for an X-linked disorder with carrier testing implications.

Eculizumab: Humanized monoclonal antibody that binds complement component C5, preventing cleavage to C5a and C5b and blocking MAC formation. First-line treatment for aHUS that has transformed outcomes, with rapid hematologic response and renal function improvement.
Ravulizumab: Long-acting C5 inhibitor engineered from eculizumab with extended dosing interval (every 8 weeks vs every 2 weeks). Non-inferior efficacy with improved convenience.

Plasma Exchange/Infusion: Historical first-line therapy before eculizumab. Plasma exchange removes mutant complement factors and autoantibodies while replacing functional complement regulators. Still used as bridging therapy and for anti-factor H antibody-mediated aHUS.

Renal Transplantation: For patients who progress to ESRD. Risk of disease recurrence in the transplanted kidney varies by genotype (high for CFH mutations, low for MCP mutations). Combined liver-kidney transplantation has been performed for CFH mutations to correct the source of mutant protein.

Immunosuppression for Anti-Factor H Antibodies: For autoimmune aHUS with anti-factor H antibodies, combination of plasma exchange with immunosuppressive therapy (mycophenolate mofetil, rituximab, or cyclophosphamide) to reduce antibody production.
Maximal Safe Surgical Resection: Surgery is used for diagnosis, decompression, and maximal cytoreduction before adjuvant therapy.
Intensive Multiagent Chemotherapy: Multiagent chemotherapy, often including high-dose chemotherapy with stem cell rescue, is a core component of contemporary AT/RT treatment protocols.

Adjuvant Radiation Therapy: Focal or craniospinal radiation is used for local and neuraxis control and remains an important component of modern AT/RT therapy despite age-related toxicity concerns in infants.

Applied Behavior Analysis: Evidence-based behavioral interventions including early intensive behavioral interventions (EIBI) and naturalistic developmental behavioral interventions (NDBI). Meta-analysis shows medium effects on intellectual functioning and adaptive behavior, but limited improvement in core ASD symptoms.

Pharmacotherapy for Associated Symptoms: No FDA-approved drugs target core ASD symptoms. Pharmacological treatments address associated symptoms such as irritability (risperidone, aripiprazole), hyperactivity, and anxiety.

Emerging Gene Therapy Approaches: CRISPR-based gene activation (CRISPRa) is emerging as a promising therapeutic approach for neurodevelopmental disorders caused by haploinsufficiency, which includes many ASD risk genes. Note: key evidence (PMID:41278953) is a preprint and has not yet undergone peer review.

Hormone Replacement Therapy: Replacement of deficient hormones is the mainstay of treatment: calcium and vitamin D for hypoparathyroidism, hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone for adrenal insufficiency, insulin for diabetes, and sex hormones for hypogonadism.
Antifungal Therapy: Chronic or intermittent antifungal therapy (fluconazole, itraconazole) for management of chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis.
Immunosuppressive Therapy: Immunosuppression (e.g., azathioprine, mycophenolate, cyclosporine) may be needed for severe autoimmune manifestations such as autoimmune hepatitis or enteropathy.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for family members given the autosomal recessive inheritance pattern. Early identification of at-risk siblings allows for surveillance and early intervention.
Coenzyme Q10 supplementation: High-dose coenzyme Q10 replacement is the main disease-targeted therapy and can improve symptoms in a substantial subset of patients.

payload.disease: Autosomal Recessive Ataxia Due to Ubiquinone Deficiency

text: Supportive multidisciplinary management: Management is symptomatic and centers on hearing rehabilitation, cataract surgery and refractive care, developmental therapies, and treatment of seizures and orthopedic or endocrine complications as needed.
Setmelanotide: MC4R agonist pharmacotherapy that bypasses upstream ciliopathy-related satiety-pathway dysfunction and reduces bodyweight in Bardet-Biedl syndrome.
Kidney transplantation: Organ transplantation is a viable treatment option for the subset of BBS patients who progress to end-stage renal disease.

Heart failure pharmacotherapy: Standard heart-failure medications are used to manage Barth cardiomyopathy, with careful attention to volume status because affected patients are vulnerable to overdiuresis and dehydration.

Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF): G-CSF is used prophylactically in neutropenic Barth syndrome patients to reduce severe bacterial infection risk and improve marrow neutrophil maturation.
Elamipretide: Elamipretide is a cardiolipin-directed mitochondrial therapy used to improve muscle strength in Barth syndrome.
Potassium Supplementation: Oral potassium chloride replacement is the cornerstone of management, often requiring high doses to maintain serum potassium above 3.0 mEq/L.
Indomethacin: Prostaglandin synthesis inhibitor (NSAID) that reduces urinary prostaglandin E2, improves electrolyte balance, and promotes growth in children with Bartter syndrome.

Potassium-Sparing Diuretics: Aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone) and direct ENaC inhibitors (amiloride) used to counteract potassium-wasting effects of secondary hyperaldosteronism. In the Korean multicenter study, 68% of patients received potassium-sparing diuretics.
Magnesium Supplementation: Oral magnesium replacement for patients with hypomagnesemia, particularly those with Types 1 and 2.

Renal Transplantation: Considered for patients who progress to end-stage renal disease, which can occur in Type 4. Transplantation corrects the electrolyte abnormalities since the defect is intrinsic to the native kidney.
Genetic Counseling: Family counseling regarding autosomal recessive or X-linked recessive inheritance patterns, carrier testing, and prenatal diagnosis options.

Dopaminergic replacement therapy: Symptomatic dopaminergic therapy can improve PFBC-associated parkinsonism, although treatment may require adjustment because motor fluctuations and dyskinesia can emerge.

Splice-switching antisense oligonucleotide therapy: Experimental ASO therapy for SLC20A2 haploinsufficiency restores functional SLC20A2 expression and suppresses calcification in a humanized mouse model.
Transurethral Resection of Bladder Tumor: Endoscopic resection for diagnosis, staging, and local control of non-muscle-invasive bladder tumors.

Intravesical Therapy: Intravesical treatment, most prominently bacillus Calmette-Guerin-based therapy, is used after endoscopic resection to reduce recurrence and progression in non-muscle-invasive disease.
Radical Cystectomy: Radical cystectomy is a standard definitive local therapy for muscle-invasive disease and for selected very high-risk or BCG-unresponsive non-muscle-invasive tumors.
Cisplatin-Based Combination Chemotherapy: Perioperative or systemic cisplatin-based chemotherapy remains a key therapy for eligible patients with muscle-invasive or metastatic disease.
Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors: PD-1/PD-L1-directed immunotherapy is used across advanced urothelial carcinoma and increasingly across earlier disease states depending on clinical context.
FGFR Inhibitor Therapy: Selected patients with FGFR2/3-altered advanced urothelial carcinoma may benefit from FGFR-targeted therapy such as erdafitinib.
Systemic Corticosteroids: First-line treatment for acute flares, particularly uveitis. Effective for short-term inflammation control but long-term use limited by side effects.
Methotrexate: Disease-modifying agent used as steroid-sparing therapy for joint and skin manifestations. Often used as first-line maintenance therapy.
Anti-TNF Biologic Therapy: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, infliximab) used for refractory disease, particularly uveitis and arthritis unresponsive to conventional DMARDs.
IL-1 Receptor Antagonist Therapy: Anakinra has shown efficacy in some refractory cases, targeting the IL-1-mediated inflammatory pathway downstream of NOD2 activation.

JAK Inhibitor Therapy: Tofacitinib, a pan-JAK inhibitor, suppresses IFN-γ-induced NOD2 expression and downstream pro-inflammatory cytokine production. Emerging treatment for refractory Blau syndrome, with clinical trials underway.

Medial canthoplasty: Surgical correction of the medial canthal complex is used to address blepharophimosis, epicanthus inversus, and telecanthus, typically as the first stage of eyelid reconstruction.

payload.disease: Blepharophimosis, Ptosis, and Epicanthus Inversus Syndrome

text: Frontalis suspension surgery: Surgical ptosis correction, including frontalis suspension approaches, is used to address co-existing blepharoptosis after medial canthal correction.

payload.disease: Blepharophimosis, Ptosis, and Epicanthus Inversus Syndrome

text: Hormone replacement therapy: Hormone replacement therapy is used to manage Type I BPES-associated premature ovarian insufficiency and hypoestrogenic sequelae.

payload.disease: Blepharophimosis, Ptosis, and Epicanthus Inversus Syndrome

text: Assisted reproductive technology: Assisted reproductive technologies are used for fertility planning and treatment in affected women with Type I BPES, although outcomes are heterogeneous across FOXL2 mutation classes.

payload.disease: Blepharophimosis, Ptosis, and Epicanthus Inversus Syndrome

text: Sun protection: Avoidance of ultraviolet exposure is recommended to reduce photosensitive skin manifestations.
Infection management: Recurrent infections should be treated aggressively because of the syndrome's immune dysfunction.
Metabolic surveillance: Ongoing monitoring for insulin resistance and diabetes is part of routine care in Bloom syndrome.
Cancer surveillance: Early identification of malignancy is a core management strategy because of the marked cancer predisposition.
Enteral feeding support: Gastrostomy or gastrojejunal tube feeding is commonly used when oral intake is inadequate because of severe feeding difficulty and cyclic emesis.

Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement: Device therapy for prevention of sudden cardiac death in high-risk Brugada syndrome, especially after malignant ventricular arrhythmia, syncope with high-risk features, or recurrent ventricular fibrillation.

Quinidine: Quinidine is used as antiarrhythmic pharmacotherapy for suppression of recurrent ventricular arrhythmia and electrical storm in Brugada syndrome, particularly when ICD therapy alone is insufficient or when ablation is not immediately available.

Epicardial substrate ablation: Epicardial ablation of the Brugada arrhythmogenic substrate is an increasingly used option for symptomatic patients with recurrent BrS-triggered ventricular arrhythmias despite ICD therapy and/or quinidine.
Immunoglobulin replacement therapy: Immunoglobulin replacement is supportive treatment for the antibody deficiency component of CD27 deficiency.

Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation: Allogeneic HSCT is the main potentially curative treatment for severe CD27-related disease, especially when lymphoma or refractory EBV-driven immune dysregulation develops.
Rituximab: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody therapy has been used as adjunctive treatment for severe EBV-driven B-cell lymphoproliferation in CD27 deficiency.

Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling is indicated for carrier parents and at-risk relatives, including discussion of autosomal recessive recurrence risk and options for prenatal molecular diagnosis in future pregnancies.

payload.disease: CHRNA1-Associated Fetal Hypo-akinesia Disorder of Prenatal Onset

text: Janus kinase inhibitor therapy: JAK inhibitors block JAK-STAT signalling downstream of the type I interferon receptor and are the most promising mechanism-targeted therapy in COPA syndrome, with published cohort-level evidence supporting baricitinib and ruxolitinib for both interstitial lung disease and arthritis.

Immunosuppression for diffuse alveolar hemorrhage: Standard induction for severe alveolar hemorrhage uses systemic glucocorticoids combined with cyclophosphamide, with subsequent maintenance using mycophenolate mofetil or azathioprine. Radiographic improvement is common but pulmonary function may continue to decline.
Lung transplantation: Lung transplantation is offered to patients with end-stage progressive interstitial lung disease refractory to immunosuppression and JAK inhibition.
Kidney transplantation: Patients who progress to advanced glomerular damage and end-stage renal disease require kidney transplantation, with outcomes documented in a multi-patient case series.

STING-directed therapy (experimental): Direct STING pathway inhibition (e.g., small-molecule inhibitors such as H-151) and STING1-directed gene therapy aimed at introducing the protective HAQ allele are emerging upstream therapeutic strategies based on the central role of Golgi-localised STING activation in COPA syndrome.

Abatacept: Abatacept is a CTLA-4-Ig fusion protein that functionally replaces the lost checkpoint signal by blocking CD80/CD86-mediated co-stimulation. It is the prototypic targeted therapy for CTLA4 haploinsufficiency and can induce rapid improvement in refractory immune dysregulation, especially enteropathy, arthritis, and lymphoproliferative disease.
