Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation can provide curative treatment for the severe immunodeficiency component of the disease.
Immunoglobulin replacement therapy: Regular IVIG prophylaxis can reduce infection burden in affected individuals with antibody deficiency.
Physical therapy: Physical therapy is a core symptomatic intervention for gait dysfunction, spasticity, and progressive disability in ARSACS.
Supportive multidisciplinary care: Ongoing neurologic, rehabilitation, and supportive multidisciplinary care is required because ARSACS remains incurable and progressively disabling.
Definitive Surgical Resection: Wide resection is the standard treatment for localized chondrosarcoma and remains the primary curative modality.
En Bloc Resection: En bloc resection with tumor-free margins is recommended for secondary peripheral chondrosarcoma arising from osteochondroma.
Chemotherapy: Systemic chemotherapy use is subtype-dependent and is most established for advanced mesenchymal chondrosarcoma rather than conventional disease.
Ivosidenib: IDH1-targeted therapy can suppress the oncometabolite program in advanced IDH1-mutant chondrosarcoma and produced durable disease control in a phase I study.

Surgical Resection: Maximal safe resection with negative margins remains the therapeutic cornerstone when anatomically feasible, although complete resection is often limited by proximity to critical neurovascular structures.
Proton Radiotherapy: High-dose proton radiotherapy is widely used as adjuvant or definitive local therapy to improve control while limiting dose to nearby critical structures.
Imatinib: Imatinib can provide disease stabilization and clinical benefit in advanced PDGFB/PDGFRB-positive chordoma and remains a commonly used targeted therapy in selected patients.
Afatinib: Afatinib is an investigational EGFR-directed therapy with broad preclinical activity across chordoma models and rationale for use in advanced disease.
Gross Total Resection: Maximal safe gross total resection is the core local therapy for CPC and the strongest surgical goal when anatomy and bleeding risk permit.
CarbEV Chemotherapy: Carboplatin-etoposide-vincristine (CarbEV/CEV) chemotherapy is an established CPC backbone and was superior to CycEV in the randomized CPC arm of CPT-SIOP-2000.

High-Dose Methotrexate-Containing Chemotherapy: High-dose methotrexate-containing induction chemotherapy can produce durable progression-free survival in young children with CPC and is used in non-myeloablative infant-focused protocols.
Craniospinal Irradiation: Radiotherapy is used in a risk-adapted way in older children with CPC. Craniospinal fields are reserved for metastatic or non-responsive disease in protocolized treatment.
Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation: Allogeneic transplantation is the only treatment with curative potential in CMML and appears most beneficial in higher-risk disease.
Hydroxyurea-Based Cytoreduction: Hydroxyurea is used for cytoreduction in proliferative CMML to control leukocytosis and disease burden, but it is not disease-modifying.
Hypomethylation Therapy: Hypomethylating therapy with azacitidine or decitabine is commonly used in CMML and yields remissions in a minority of patients, without reliably erasing the mutant clone.
ABVD-Based Chemotherapy: Combination chemotherapy with ABVD remains a standard systemic treatment backbone for Hodgkin lymphoma.
Radiation Therapy: Radiotherapy is used with chemotherapy in selected Hodgkin lymphoma treatment strategies.
Brentuximab Vedotin: CD30-directed antibody-drug conjugate therapy has activity in relapsed or refractory classic Hodgkin lymphoma and is commonly combined with chemotherapy.
Pembrolizumab: PD-1 blockade with pembrolizumab is active in relapsed or refractory classic Hodgkin lymphoma.
Nivolumab: PD-1 blockade with nivolumab is an established treatment option in previously treated classic Hodgkin lymphoma and is tightly aligned to the disease's 9p24.1-driven checkpoint biology.

First-Line Platinum-Taxane Chemotherapy: Carboplatin plus paclitaxel remains the standard first-line chemotherapy backbone for OCCC, even though advanced-stage disease often shows relative platinum resistance and the regimen remains imperfect for this histology.
Symptomatic Nail Care: Regular trimming, filing, and protective measures for dystrophic nails. No disease-modifying treatment is currently available for HED.
Dermatologic Management of Keratoderma: Topical keratolytics (urea, salicylic acid) and emollients for palmoplantar hyperkeratosis.

Hemichannel-Blocking Antibody Therapy (Preclinical): The monoclonal antibody abEC1.1, which blocks connexin hemichannels, has shown preclinical promise in a Cx30-A88V mouse model of Clouston syndrome. Topical and systemic administration reduced skin cell proliferation and sebaceous gland size, supporting hemichannel blockade as a candidate therapeutic strategy.
Wig Use for Alopecia: Wigs or hairpieces for cosmetic management of alopecia.
Genetic Counseling: Counseling regarding autosomal dominant inheritance pattern and recurrence risk. Prenatal diagnosis is possible through identification of known family mutations in GJB6.

Supportive multidisciplinary care: Current management is supportive and includes developmental therapies, orthopedic monitoring, seizure management, and surveillance for cardiac and neurologic complications.
Parent management training: Parent management training is an evidence-supported psychosocial treatment for children with clinical levels of disruptive behavior.
Parent-child interaction therapy: Parent-child interaction therapy is a parent-child psychosocial treatment with large waitlist-controlled effects in young children with disruptive behavior.

Specialized modules for callous-unemotional traits: Children with disruptive behavior disorders and callous-unemotional traits can improve with treatment, but higher severity supports specialized modules alongside parenting programs.

Ziprasidone pharmacotherapy for severe aggression: Ziprasidone has been studied as adjunctive symptom-targeted pharmacotherapy for severe conduct and disruptive behavior disorders in older children and adolescents.
Sucrose- and starch-restricted diet: Dietary restriction of sucrose and, when needed, starch is first-line management and should be individualized to symptom tolerance.
Sacrosidase enzyme replacement therapy: Oral sacrosidase can supplement deficient sucrase activity, although some patients still need ongoing starch restriction.
Supportive heart failure therapy: Standard heart-failure therapy with ACE inhibitors, beta blockers, diuretics, and digoxin is used to stabilize patients with milder dilated cardiomyopathy.
Heart transplantation: Cardiac transplantation can be life-saving for progressive or refractory DK1-CDG cardiomyopathy.
Supportive neurodevelopmental and behavioral care: Management is supportive and typically includes developmental therapies, educational support, and symptom-guided neurologic or behavioral treatment.

Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation: Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation is the disease-modifying therapy for marrow failure, myelodysplasia, or leukemia arising in GATA2 deficiency.
Speech and Language Therapy: Targets communication deficits through speech, sign language, or augmentative alternative communication devices.
Physical Therapy: Stretching, strengthening, and mobility training to address gross motor delay and spasticity.
Cochlear Implantation: Direct stimulation of the auditory nerve for patients with sensorineural hearing loss.
Hearing Aid Usage: Acoustic amplification for patients with mild to moderate sensorineural hearing loss.
Genetic Counseling: Risk assessment and family planning. Recurrence risk is extremely low (<1/200) as cases arise from de novo mutations.
Supportive Care: Multidisciplinary management including feeding support, respiratory management, and seizure control. No disease-modifying therapies are currently available.
Antiseizure medication therapy: Antiseizure medications are used for seizure management in affected individuals.

Heat Avoidance and Environmental Modification: Primary management involves environmental modifications to prevent hyperthermia, including heat avoidance, cooling vests, access to adequate water supply, and education about heat-related risks. Exposure to extreme heat should be avoided.

Dental Implantation and Prosthodontic Rehabilitation: Prosthodontic management including dentures, dental implants, and orthodontic treatment to address oligodontia and conical teeth. Early dental treatment by age one year with follow-up every six to twelve months is recommended.
Genetic Counseling: Autosomal recessive inheritance counseling for affected families. Carrier testing and prenatal/preimplantation genetic testing are available when the familial variants are known.

Symptomatic Management of Hypohidrosis: Environmental modifications to prevent overheating including cooling vests, air conditioning, and avoidance of excessive physical exertion in heat. Access to adequate water supply and cool environments during hot weather is essential.

Dental Rehabilitation: Prosthetic dental treatment including removable dentures in childhood, bonding of conical teeth, orthodontics, and dental implants in adulthood to address oligodontia and abnormal tooth morphology.

Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for families to determine inheritance pattern (AR vs AD) and recurrence risk. Identification of the specific EDAR pathogenic variant enables prenatal and preimplantation genetic testing.

Pancreatic enzyme replacement or supplementation therapy: Oral pancreatic enzyme replacement with fat-soluble vitamin supplementation is used to manage exocrine pancreatic insufficiency in the SDS spectrum.

payload.treatment_name: Pancreatic enzyme replacement or supplementation therapy

text: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor therapy: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor pharmacotherapy can be used for severe recurrent infection with persistent severe neutropenia in the SDS spectrum.
Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation is considered for severe bone marrow failure or myeloid malignant transformation in the SDS spectrum.

Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation: Allogeneic HSCT is the definitive treatment for the immunodeficiency component, but outcomes are poor with high transplant-related mortality. Five of eleven transplanted patients survived in a 2017 review, with four requiring continued prophylaxis. Poor outcomes are partly attributed to the non-hematopoietic (stromal) cell contribution to immunodeficiency, as mutant IkBa in stromal cells impairs lymphorganogenesis even after successful donor cell engraftment.

Antimicrobial Prophylaxis: Prophylactic antimicrobials are essential for patients who do not undergo HSCT or who have incomplete immune reconstitution post-transplant. Includes antibacterial, antifungal, and Pneumocystis prophylaxis.
Immunoglobulin Replacement Therapy: Intravenous or subcutaneous immunoglobulin replacement for patients with agammaglobulinemia or hypogammaglobulinemia.

Total Hysterectomy with Bilateral Salpingo-Oophorectomy: Standard primary treatment for most surgically eligible patients with endometrial carcinoma, usually performed via minimally invasive approaches when feasible.

Carboplatin Plus Paclitaxel Chemotherapy: Carboplatin plus paclitaxel is the global first-line chemotherapy backbone for advanced or recurrent endometrial carcinoma and serves as the chemotherapy partner in modern first-line immunotherapy combinations.

Pembrolizumab Plus Carboplatin-Paclitaxel: Pembrolizumab added to paclitaxel-carboplatin improves progression-free survival for advanced or recurrent endometrial carcinoma, with benefit in both dMMR and pMMR cohorts.
Lenvatinib Plus Pembrolizumab: Combination systemic therapy for advanced endometrial carcinoma after prior platinum-based chemotherapy, with survival benefit over physician's-choice chemotherapy.

Dostarlimab Plus Carboplatin-Paclitaxel: Dostarlimab added to carboplatin-paclitaxel improves progression-free survival in primary advanced or recurrent endometrial cancer, with the largest benefit in the dMMR/MSI-H population.

Trastuzumab Plus Carboplatin-Paclitaxel for HER2-Positive Uterine Serous Carcinoma: HER2/Neu-positive uterine serous carcinoma, an aggressive endometrial carcinoma subtype, may be treated with trastuzumab added to carboplatin-paclitaxel.

payload.treatment_name: Trastuzumab Plus Carboplatin-Paclitaxel for HER2-Positive Uterine Serous Carcinoma

text: Fertility-Sparing High-Dose Progestin Therapy: Selected patients with grade 1, stage I endometrioid disease who desire fertility preservation may be managed with high-dose progestin and close endometrial surveillance.
Glucocorticoid therapy: Systemic corticosteroids remain foundational remission-induction therapy across EGPA phenotypes.
Mepolizumab: Anti-IL-5 therapy reduces eosinophilic activity, steroid burden, and relapse frequency in relapsing or refractory EGPA.
Rituximab: Anti-CD20 therapy is used for remission induction in vasculitic EGPA and is being evaluated against conventional induction strategies in randomized clinical trials.
Acetazolamide: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor; first-line prophylactic therapy for EA2, reducing attack frequency and severity. Variable benefit in EA1.
4-Aminopyridine: Potassium channel blocker used as prophylactic therapy for EA2; restores Purkinje cell firing precision.
Carbamazepine: Sodium channel blocker anti-seizure medication used off-label as symptomatic therapy for EA1 attacks. Acetazolamide is generally less effective in EA1 than in EA2.
Acitretin: Systemic retinoid therapy may substantially improve hyperkeratosis in some patients, but treatment response is variable and resistance has also been reported.
Endoscopic Therapy for T1a Disease: T1a esophageal adenocarcinoma can be managed endoscopically in carefully staged patients, preserving the esophagus while treating superficial neoplasia.
Esophagectomy: Esophagectomy remains a core treatment for resectable invasive disease and is often used for locally advanced tumors in combination with neoadjuvant therapy.
Neoadjuvant CROSS Chemoradiotherapy Plus Surgery: For locally advanced resectable disease, carboplatin-paclitaxel based neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy followed by surgery improves long-term survival.
Adjuvant Nivolumab: In resected esophageal or gastroesophageal junction cancer with residual pathologic disease after neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy, adjuvant nivolumab prolongs disease-free survival.
Esophagectomy: Surgical resection is a core curative-intent treatment for resectable localized ESCC, typically used in multimodality management.

Definitive Chemoradiation: Concurrent chemoradiation is a standard organ-preserving approach for locally advanced or unresectable localized ESCC and is also used as part of neoadjuvant therapy strategies.

Nivolumab: Anti-PD-1 immunotherapy used in advanced or previously treated ESCC. It improves overall survival compared with chemotherapy in the post-platinum setting and is now part of the immunotherapy treatment landscape for ESCC.
Pembrolizumab-Based Therapy: Pembrolizumab-containing regimens are used in advanced ESCC, particularly in biomarker-selected settings and combination regimens involving chemotherapy.

Sirolimus: mTOR inhibition with sirolimus is an effective steroid-sparing therapy for refractory autoimmune cytopenias and benign lymphoproliferation in ALPS, and can normalize the double-negative T-cell compartment.

Mycophenolate mofetil: Mycophenolate mofetil is an established steroid-sparing immunosuppressive therapy for chronic autoimmune disease manifestations in ALPS, particularly when long-term corticosteroids should be avoided.
Aerobic Exercise Training: Regular endurance training can improve fitness, walking speed, and self-assessed health without evidence of exercise-induced muscle damage.
Scapulopexy: Scapular fixation surgery can improve shoulder range of motion and reduce disabling winging in appropriately selected patients.

Pain Management and Supportive Care: Pain-directed pharmacologic and nonpharmacologic management, assistive devices, and multidisciplinary rehabilitation remain central to current care because pain is common and materially affects quality of life.
Growth hormone therapy: Recombinant human growth hormone has been used for short stature, but published evidence suggests only limited benefit in Floating-Harbor syndrome.
Developmental supportive care: Early intervention, special education, and related supportive services are recommended for developmental disability management.
Speech and communication therapy: Communication rehabilitation is recommended for the severe expressive and receptive language phenotype.

Corticosteroids: Prednisone remains standard initial immunosuppressive therapy for primary FSGS with nephrotic syndrome after secondary and monogenic causes have been excluded. Some patients achieve partial or complete remission, but steroid resistance is common.

Calcineurin Inhibitors: Cyclosporine or tacrolimus are established options for steroid-resistant primary FSGS. Their benefit likely reflects both immunomodulation and direct stabilization of podocyte structure.

Sparsentan: Dual endothelin type A and angiotensin II type 1 receptor antagonist that lowers proteinuria in selected patients with FSGS. Its clearest current role is as an antiproteinuric therapy rather than reversal of established glomerular scarring.

Renin-Angiotensin System Blockade: ACE inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers are core supportive therapy to lower intraglomerular pressure, reduce proteinuria, and treat hypertension across primary and secondary FSGS.
Mycophenolate Mofetil: Steroid-sparing immunosuppressive option sometimes used in selected primary FSGS regimens. Evidence is stronger for proteinuria reduction than for consistent disease remission.

Kidney Transplantation: Kidney transplantation is replacement therapy for FSGS-associated kidney failure rather than disease-modifying therapy for the native disease. Recurrence risk is highest in primary FSGS and is generally lower in many monogenic or maladaptive forms.

Anti-CD20-Based Immunochemotherapy: Standard therapy for symptomatic advanced FL combines an anti-CD20 antibody such as rituximab or obinutuzumab with chemotherapy backbones such as bendamustine, CHOP, or CVP, depending on patient fitness and disease burden.
Lenalidomide Plus Anti-CD20 Therapy: The chemo-free lenalidomide plus rituximab regimen is an established option for selected patients in frontline or relapsed settings.

Tazemetostat: Oral EZH2 inhibition with tazemetostat is used in relapsed or refractory FL, especially in tumors with EZH2 mutation, and offers a targeted option with relatively favorable tolerability.
Genetic counseling: Early molecular diagnosis supports family counseling, recurrence-risk assessment, and prenatal testing or imaging in future pregnancies.

Kidney transplantation: Selected children who survive to end-stage kidney disease may undergo kidney transplantation, but this treats only the renal component and reported success is subtype-dependent.
Physical therapy: Physical therapy is used to preserve mobility and function in myopathic GYG1 deficiency.
Supportive care: Supportive management includes symptom-directed follow-up, physical therapy, and cardiac surveillance for muscle and cardiac complications.
Sirolimus therapy: mTOR inhibition with sirolimus is used in progressive disease to suppress lymphatic proliferation and reduce complications.
Bisphosphonate therapy: Bisphosphonates are commonly used to reduce osteolysis and skeletal complications in GSD.
Surgical and interventional management: Selected patients require stabilization, pleural procedures, or resection for site-specific complications.
Purine nucleoside analog therapy: Symptomatic first-line classic HCL is treated with purine nucleoside analogs, most commonly cladribine or pentostatin.
Cladribine plus rituximab: Combining rituximab with first-line cladribine improves MRD-free complete remission depth and durability compared with cladribine alone.

Vemurafenib plus rituximab: For relapsed or refractory classic HCL, combined BRAF inhibition and anti-CD20 therapy achieves high complete-response and MRD-negative response rates without myelotoxic chemotherapy.
Moxetumomab pasudotox: CD22-directed immunotoxin therapy is an active option for multiply pretreated relapsed or refractory HCL.
Immunoglobulin replacement therapy: Intravenous or subcutaneous immunoglobulin replacement improves survival and infection control in affected infants.
Defibrotide: Defibrotide is used for acute hepatic disease and prophylactically around HSCT in VODI.
Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation: Early HSCT with non-hepatotoxic conditioning is a potentially curative treatment strategy for VODI.
Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis: Prophylaxis against Pneumocystis jirovecii is part of standard supportive care for affected infants with profound combined immunodeficiency.
Liver transplantation: Liver transplantation may be considered in severe hepatic failure, although complications can be substantial in this immunodeficient syndrome.
Cisplatin-Based Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy: Cisplatin-based chemotherapy is standard for most unresectable or advanced tumors and is used preoperatively to shrink disease and improve resectability.
Complete Surgical Resection: Complete extirpation of residual tumor remains essential for cure and is integrated with chemotherapy in most patients.

Liver Transplantation for Unresectable Liver-Confined Disease: Orthotopic liver transplantation is a curative option for unresectable unifocal or multifocal tumors confined to the liver and should be considered early when PRETEXT extent, vascular involvement, or low AFP suggest failed conservative surgery.

payload.treatment_name: Liver Transplantation for Unresectable Liver-Confined Disease

text: Fructose-, sucrose-, and sorbitol-restricted diet: Absolute avoidance of fructose, sucrose, and sorbitol is the standard lifelong treatment and usually prevents acute metabolic toxicity when started and maintained consistently.

Ketohexokinase inhibition (investigational): Ketohexokinase-directed therapy is a preclinical, mechanism-based strategy intended to reduce fructose-1-phosphate generation upstream of the blocked aldolase B step. No approved KHK-targeted therapy for HFI currently exists.
Physical therapy: Physical therapy is used symptomatically to support gross motor recovery, strength, gait safety, and daily function after focal neuropathic episodes.

payload.disease: Hereditary Neuropathy with Liability to Pressure Palsies

text: Occupational therapy: Occupational therapy is used for fine motor recovery, hand function, and activities of daily living, especially after upper-limb entrapment neuropathies.

payload.disease: Hereditary Neuropathy with Liability to Pressure Palsies

text: Bracing and orthotic support: Wrist splints and ankle-foot orthoses are used when focal weakness such as hand weakness or foot drop needs transient or ongoing mechanical support.

payload.disease: Hereditary Neuropathy with Liability to Pressure Palsies

text: Analgesic pharmacotherapy for neuropathic pain: No disease-modifying drug therapy is established; when neuropathic pain is present, symptomatic analgesic medication can be used.

payload.disease: Hereditary Neuropathy with Liability to Pressure Palsies

text: Compression protection and vincristine avoidance: Preventive management includes padding vulnerable compression sites, avoiding repetitive or prolonged nerve compression, and avoiding vincristine because it can worsen neuropathy.

payload.disease: Hereditary Neuropathy with Liability to Pressure Palsies

text: Idursulfase enzyme replacement therapy: Intravenous recombinant iduronate-2-sulfatase is the standard disease-modifying therapy for peripheral somatic disease. It lowers urinary glycosaminoglycans, reduces organomegaly, and can improve endurance, but treatment effects are incomplete and CNS disease is not corrected.

Multidisciplinary supportive care: Hunter syndrome remains a high-burden multisystem disease even in treated adults, so ongoing respiratory, ENT/audiologic, orthopedic, cardiology, and surgical management is required alongside disease-directed therapy.

Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation: Early hematopoietic stem cell transplantation is the standard disease-modifying therapy for severe Hurler syndrome and offers the best chance to preserve neurocognitive function.
Enzyme replacement therapy: Intravenous laronidase improves somatic storage manifestations in Hurler syndrome but does not adequately treat central nervous system disease.
Corticosteroid therapy: Systemic glucocorticoids remain first-line therapy for many patients because they rapidly suppress eosinophilia and clinical manifestations.

Imatinib for PDGFRA-rearranged HES: Tyrosine-kinase inhibition is particularly effective in patients with FIP1L1-PDGFRA-associated disease and is used as precision therapy for the clonal myeloid subset.

Anti-IL-5 or anti-IL-5R biologic therapy: Targeted monoclonal antibodies against the IL-5 axis are used for severe or relapsing disease and are especially relevant when eosinophilia remains corticosteroid-dependent or corticosteroid-refractory.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for affected families and carriers, particularly important given the high prevalence in consanguineous populations.

Ophthalmological Surveillance: Regular ophthalmological monitoring for all patients with congenital hypotrichosis, as macular changes may precede visual symptoms. Serial retinal imaging (OCT, fundus autofluorescence) is important to track disease progression.
Low Vision Aids: Supportive management of progressive central visual loss with low vision aids and rehabilitation. No curative or disease-modifying therapy currently exists for the retinal dystrophy.

Gene Therapy (Investigational): Retinal cadherinopathies including HJMD are considered potentially treatable with molecular therapeutic approaches. Gene replacement or gene editing strategies targeting CDH3 in the RPE are under investigation as future therapeutic directions, though no clinical trials are currently underway for HJMD specifically.
Immunoglobulin Replacement Therapy: Intravenous or subcutaneous immunoglobulin replacement for hypogammaglobulinemia in EDA-ID patients.
Antimicrobial Prophylaxis: Prophylactic antibiotics and antifungals to prevent recurrent infections in immunodeficient patients.

Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation: HSCT is the only curative treatment for the immunodeficiency component of EDA-ID, though it does not correct ectodermal features. Outcomes are variable, with significant transplant-related morbidity.
Supportive Dermatological Care: Management of skin lesions in IP including topical treatments and monitoring for secondary infections during the vesicular stage.
Pirfenidone: Oral antifibrotic therapy that slows physiologic decline and improves progression-free survival in IPF.
Nintedanib: Antifibrotic tyrosine kinase inhibitor that slows the rate of forced vital capacity decline in IPF.

Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation: Only curative therapy for the bone marrow failure component. Reduced-intensity conditioning regimens have improved outcomes in FA and DC patients. In TBDs, toxicities of the conditioning regimen can be detrimental to other organs.
Androgen Therapy: Oxymetholone and other androgens can temporarily improve blood counts, particularly in FA and TBDs. Androgens may upregulate telomerase activity.
Corticosteroid Therapy: First-line treatment for Diamond-Blackfan anemia. The 2024 international consensus recommends lowering the maintenance dose to a maximum of 0.3 mg/kg per day.
Supportive Care: Transfusion support with iron chelation therapy for transfusion-dependent patients. Growth factor support (G-CSF, EPO) in selected cases.
Cancer Screening: Regular screening for MDS/AML and solid tumors given elevated cancer risk across all subtypes. Bone marrow surveillance can detect premalignant clones in SDS.

Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation: Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation remains the only curative therapy for most children with canonical JMML, although relapse and regimen-related toxicity remain major limitations.

Azacitidine: Hypomethylating therapy used before transplantation to control disease burden and improve clinical status in newly diagnosed JMML. It is particularly useful as bridge therapy while definitive transplant is being arranged.
Trametinib: MEK inhibitor with demonstrated activity in relapsed or refractory JMML and growing use as a bridge to HSCT or prolonged disease-control therapy in selected patients.

Symptomatic Management: No specific treatment exists for the underlying keratin defect. Management is supportive and includes gentle hair care to minimize breakage, nail care, and cosmetic interventions such as wigs.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for autosomal recessive inheritance pattern, carrier testing for family members, and prenatal diagnosis when the familial mutations are known.

Supportive multidisciplinary management: Management is supportive and may include hearing rehabilitation, nutritional support, airway and pulmonary management, and treatment of gastrointestinal complications.
Pharmacotherapy for psychosis: Low-dose aripiprazole can be used to control psychotic symptoms in Kufor-Rakeb syndrome without major motor worsening in at least isolated reported cases.
Multidisciplinary supportive care: Management relies on genetic diagnosis, counseling, and multidisciplinary supportive care for progressive motor and cognitive disability.
Abatacept: CTLA4-Ig replacement therapy that addresses the checkpoint defect caused by LRBA loss. It is particularly useful for immune dysregulation, lymphoproliferation, and chronic enteropathy.
Immunoglobulin replacement therapy: Supportive IVIG or SCIG replacement is commonly used to manage the humoral immune deficiency and recurrent infections associated with LRBA deficiency.

Allogeneic stem cell transplantation: Allogeneic HSCT can provide durable immune reconstitution and remission in selected patients with severe or refractory disease, although transplant risk remains substantial.

Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling is indicated for affected families because LRBA deficiency is a recessive Mendelian disorder that may recur in siblings when both parents carry pathogenic LRBA variants.

Guideline-directed heart failure pharmacotherapy: No disease-modifying therapy reverses LVNC, so treatment focuses on heart failure symptom control with standard pharmacologic regimens such as ACE inhibitors and beta blockers when ventricular dysfunction is present.

Anticoagulation therapy for thromboembolic risk: Anticoagulation is considered when LVNC patients have atrial fibrillation, left ventricular dysfunction, prior embolism, or other high-risk features that increase the probability of intracardiac stasis and embolization.

Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement: ICD placement is used for primary or secondary sudden-death prevention in patients with severe systolic dysfunction or malignant ventricular arrhythmia risk.
Heart transplantation: Transplantation is reserved for end-stage disease when heart failure or refractory complications progress despite maximal medical and device therapy.
Surgical Resection: Complete surgical resection is the main curative-intent treatment for localized leiomyosarcoma and should be planned in experienced sarcoma centers.
Radiation Therapy: Radiation is commonly used as an adjunct to surgery for local control, particularly when achieving adequate margins is difficult.
Anthracycline-Based Chemotherapy: Doxorubicin-based chemotherapy remains a standard first-line systemic option for metastatic or unresectable leiomyosarcoma.

Gemcitabine-Based Chemotherapy: Gemcitabine-based regimens are also used in metastatic or unresectable leiomyosarcoma, particularly when anthracycline therapy is unsuitable or after prior treatment exposure.
Supportive multidisciplinary care: Ongoing orthopedic and dental or maxillofacial follow-up is recommended to manage the progressive skeletal complications of the disorder.
Beta-Blocker Therapy: First-line anti-adrenergic therapy for most patients with inherited long QT syndrome.
Left Cardiac Sympathetic Denervation: Surgical anti-adrenergic escalation when recurrent syncope or major events persist despite full-dose beta-blockade.
Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator Placement: Device therapy for high-risk patients, especially after cardiac arrest or when malignant events recur despite optimal medical therapy.
Mexiletine: Precision pharmacotherapy most relevant to the SCN5A/LQT3 subtype, where late sodium current blockade shortens QTc and lowers arrhythmic event burden.
Cataract extraction: Early cataract surgery is part of standard management to clear the visual axis in affected infants.
Glaucoma surgery: Surgical pressure-lowering procedures are frequently required for glaucoma, with aqueous tube shunts performing well in retrospective survey data.
Renal tubular loss replacement: Supportive replacement of bicarbonate, phosphate, potassium, water, and related losses is used to correct tubular acidosis and bone disease.
Hormone replacement therapy: Estrogen-based hormone replacement is used to treat hypoestrogenism in the ovarian-failure branch, support pubertal development, and reduce osteoporosis risk.

Gastrointestinal cancer surveillance: Because biallelic MCM9 deficiency confers polyposis plus gastric and early-onset colorectal cancer risk, affected individuals may benefit from structured gastrointestinal surveillance including colonoscopic and endoscopic screening.

Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling is appropriate for affected families because the condition is recessive, causes infertility, and carries implications for cancer surveillance and reproductive planning.
Speech and Language Therapy: Early speech and language therapy is recommended to address the prominent communication delays that are among the most consistent features of MED13 syndrome.

Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling is recommended for affected families. Most cases are de novo but parent-to-child transmission has been documented, necessitating parental testing and recurrence risk assessment.
Supportive Care: Multidisciplinary supportive care including occupational therapy, physical therapy, educational support, and behavioral interventions.
Zinc acetate pharmacotherapy: Zinc acetate therapy has been proposed to treat liver copper overload in MEDNIK syndrome.
Intravenous immunoglobulin replacement therapy: Intravenous immunoglobulin replacement was used to manage persistent hypogammaglobulinemia in the reported MGAT2-CDG case.

Hydroxocobalamin therapy: Parenteral hydroxocobalamin is the central disease-modifying therapy for cblC deficiency. Higher-dose regimens are associated with better biochemical and neurodevelopmental outcomes in early-onset disease and with rapid metabolic correction in late-onset adults.

payload.disease: MMACHC-related Methylmalonic Aciduria and Homocystinuria, cblC Type

text: Betaine supplementation: Betaine is used as adjunctive remethylation therapy together with hydroxocobalamin-based regimens, particularly in late-onset or severe disease.

payload.disease: MMACHC-related Methylmalonic Aciduria and Homocystinuria, cblC Type

text: Newborn screening: Presymptomatic identification through newborn screening improves outcome, especially when treatment begins within the first days of life.

payload.disease: MMACHC-related Methylmalonic Aciduria and Homocystinuria, cblC Type

text: Ophthalmologic monitoring: Close serial ophthalmologic examination is warranted because retinal degeneration may begin in the first months of life before obvious visual deprivation.

payload.disease: MMACHC-related Methylmalonic Aciduria and Homocystinuria, cblC Type

text: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy: CBT may be used to treat anxiety and ADHD. Symptomatic aids such as colored glasses and low voice tone may reduce anxiety outbursts.
Developmental Support: Standard treatment for developmental delay and intellectual disability including speech therapy, occupational therapy, and educational support.
Antiepileptic Medication: Standard treatment for seizures and epilepsy in affected individuals.
Cardiovascular Surveillance: Annual cardiology follow-up is recommended given the risk of aortic root dilatation.
Eculizumab: Terminal complement inhibition is used in severe systemic disease, especially when gastrointestinal involvement is present.
Treprostinil: Prostacyclin analogue therapy is paired with complement inhibition to improve vascular patency and reduce relapse risk.
Wide Surgical Resection: Surgery with negative margins is the chief curative treatment for localized MPNST.
Radiation Therapy: Radiotherapy is used for local control, especially in high-grade or margin- threatening disease, although benefit appears context-dependent.

Ifosfamide-Based Chemotherapy: Anthracycline/ifosfamide-based chemotherapy remains a sarcoma-style systemic treatment option for advanced, metastatic, or downstaging settings, but the benefit is still uncertain and no targeted standard of care exists.
Cytoreductive Surgery: Upfront maximal cytoreduction is favored when complete or near-complete resection is achievable in carefully selected patients.

Hyperthermic Intraperitoneal Chemotherapy: HIPEC is paired with cytoreductive surgery in carefully selected resectable diffuse peritoneal mesothelioma and is part of the accepted locoregional standard-of-care strategy.
Pemetrexed Plus Cisplatin: Platinum-antifolate chemotherapy is an active systemic option for unresectable disease and for patients who are not candidates for curative locoregional treatment.
Atezolizumab Plus Bevacizumab: Combined PD-L1 and VEGF blockade has shown activity in advanced unresectable malignant peritoneal mesothelioma after prior platinum-pemetrexed chemotherapy.
Diaphragmatic Hernia Repair: Surgical repair of congenital diaphragmatic defects is a core management intervention for affected individuals with Meacham syndrome.

Prophylactic Gonadectomy: In WT1-related disorders with testicular developmental abnormalities, prophylactic gonadectomy is used to reduce gonadoblastoma risk and may be relevant to Meacham syndrome patients with marked gonadal dysgenesis.
Multidisciplinary DSD Care: Management of WT1-related disorders with gonadal and genital anomalies requires coordinated care involving genetics, endocrinology, urology, and psychological support.
Rituximab: Anti-CD20 B-cell depletion used in guideline-supported first-line immunotherapy for primary membranous nephropathy.
Cyclophosphamide-based immunosuppression: Cyclophosphamide-based regimens remain standard high-risk therapy for primary membranous nephropathy.
Supportive CKD therapy: Supportive therapy aimed at reducing non-immune kidney injury progression, including renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system blockade and SGLT2 inhibition.
NSAIDs or aspirin during aura: Early anti-inflammatory treatment during the aura phase is used to blunt or reduce the subsequent headache phase rather than to abort aura itself.
Triptan at headache onset: Triptans are recommended after aura when headache begins, especially if early anti-inflammatory therapy is insufficient.
Topiramate prophylaxis: Topiramate is used as preventive therapy in migraine with aura and has comparable short-term efficacy to propranolol in randomized clinical study.
Propranolol prophylaxis: Propranolol remains a preventive option for migraine with aura with similar trial-level efficacy to topiramate in direct comparison.
CGRP-pathway monoclonal antibody prophylaxis: Monoclonal antibodies targeting the CGRP pathway are effective preventive therapies for migraine, including migraine with aura.

Corticosteroid Therapy: First-line treatment for MCD. Adult cohorts show high remission rates after corticosteroid induction, but frequent relapses drive use of steroid- sparing strategies in frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent disease.

Rituximab: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody used for frequently relapsing or steroid- dependent MCD. Adult retrospective data show a 78% overall response rate, but relapse remains common and optimal dosing is still evolving.

Calcineurin Inhibitors: Cyclosporine and tacrolimus are used as steroid-sparing agents for frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent MCD. Combined with reduced-dose prednisolone, they achieve similar remission rates with fewer steroid toxicities and may also stabilize the podocyte actin cytoskeleton.

Cyclophosphamide: Alkylating immunosuppressive therapy used as a steroid-sparing option for frequently relapsing, steroid-dependent, or steroid-resistant adult MCD when relapse burden or steroid toxicity justifies escalation.

Mycophenolate Therapy: Mycophenolate-based immunosuppression, including mycophenolate mofetil and enteric-coated mycophenolate sodium formulations, is used as a steroid- sparing option in frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent adult MCD and as salvage therapy in selected resistant cases.

Type A elosulfase alfa enzyme replacement therapy: Weekly intravenous recombinant GALNS replaces the deficient Type A enzyme, lowers storage biomarkers, and improves endurance, respiratory function, and growth-related outcomes. Skeletal, tracheal, and ocular tissues remain only partially responsive because of limited penetration.

payload.treatment_name: Type A elosulfase alfa enzyme replacement therapy

text: Cervical decompression and fusion: Surgical stabilization of the craniovertebral junction is used when odontoid hypoplasia and atlantoaxial instability threaten the spinal cord.
Airway surgery for tracheal obstruction: Tracheal reconstructive procedures or tracheotomy may be required for life-threatening airway obstruction in severe disease.

Physical therapy and mobility support: Rehabilitation, pain control, and mobility aids help preserve function and reduce disability from chronic skeletal disease, even though they do not correct the underlying lysosomal defect.
Anti-seizure pharmacotherapy: Anti-seizure medications are commonly used in Mowat-Wilson syndrome, with levetiracetam and valproic acid showing benefit in the reported epilepsy cohort.
Supportive multidisciplinary care: Management is symptom-directed and typically requires coordinated developmental, neurologic, gastrointestinal, and cardiac care.
Narrowband UVB Phototherapy: Narrowband UVB is a skin-directed option for early-stage MF, particularly stage IA-IB patch or plaque disease.
Total Skin Electron Beam Radiation Therapy: Total skin electron beam therapy is used for diffuse cutaneous disease and remains a core MF radiotherapeutic modality.

payload.treatment_name: Total Skin Electron Beam Radiation Therapy

text: Mogamulizumab: CCR4-directed antibody therapy that improves progression-free survival in previously treated cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and is relevant to relapsed/refractory MF.
Brentuximab Vedotin: CD30-directed antibody-drug conjugate with superior durable response over physician's choice in previously treated CD30-positive cutaneous T-cell lymphoma.
Vorinostat: Histone deacetylase inhibition with vorinostat provides disease control and symptom relief in persistent, progressive, or treatment-refractory MF.
Supportive mitochondrial disease management: Management is supportive and includes symptom-based neurologic, ophthalmologic, and rehabilitation care.
Physical therapy: Rehabilitation is used to preserve mobility, balance, and function in progressive neuromuscular disease.
Eculizumab: Anti-C5 complement blockade directly targets the complement-dependent astrocytopathic mechanism of anti-AQP4 NMOSD and reduces relapse risk.

payload.disease: Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder with Anti-AQP4 Antibodies

text: Inebilizumab: Anti-CD19 B-lineage depletion reduces the cellular source of pathogenic AQP4 autoantibody production and lowers attack risk.

payload.disease: Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder with Anti-AQP4 Antibodies

text: Satralizumab: IL-6 receptor blockade interferes with the cytokine-amplification loop that supports plasmablast survival and Th17-skewed inflammation in anti-AQP4 disease.

payload.disease: Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder with Anti-AQP4 Antibodies

text: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation may be considered in selected patients with severe immunodeficiency or related complications.

Dental Prosthetics and Implants: Prosthetic rehabilitation is the mainstay of treatment for oligodontia. Options include removable dentures, fixed bridges, and dental implants (after skeletal maturity).

Supportive Dermatologic Care: Emollient application for xerosis, keratolytic agents for palmoplantar keratoderma, and monitoring of skin lesions. Skin manifestations may be misdiagnosed as psoriasis and require appropriate ectodermal dysplasia-specific management.
Genetic Counseling: Recommended for affected families given autosomal recessive inheritance and the high carrier frequency of WNT10A pathogenic variants in European populations.

Cardiac surveillance with echocardiography and electrocardiographic monitoring: Individuals with Ogden syndrome should undergo structured cardiac evaluation, including echocardiography for structural defects and ECG or Holter monitoring for electrophysiologic abnormalities and QT prolongation.

payload.treatment_name: Cardiac surveillance with echocardiography and electrocardiographic monitoring

text: Speech therapy: Speech therapy is commonly used in Ogden syndrome and showed the clearest signal for benefit among the non-pharmacologic therapies assessed in the prospective cohort.
Physical therapy: Physical therapy is frequently used as supportive management for hypotonia and motor impairment in Ogden syndrome.
Parent management training: Parent management training and behavioral parent training are core psychosocial interventions for reducing ODD and disruptive behavior symptoms.
Child-directed cognitive behavioral therapy: Child-directed CBT can be used for school-aged children with ODD, including Coping Power Program-style problem-solving and emotion-regulation components.

Atomoxetine pharmacotherapy for ADHD with comorbid ODD: Atomoxetine has been studied as pharmacotherapy in children and adolescents with ADHD and comorbid ODD; this treatment context targets ADHD with comorbid oppositional symptoms rather than ODD alone.

payload.treatment_name: Atomoxetine pharmacotherapy for ADHD with comorbid ODD

text: Supportive multidisciplinary care: Management requires coordinated neurologic, audiologic, gastrointestinal, rehabilitation, and developmental support.
Surgery for Hirschsprung disease: Definitive bowel surgery is required when aganglionosis causes obstructive intestinal disease.
Supportive multidisciplinary care: Management is supportive and includes neurologic, low-vision, audiologic, and rehabilitation interventions.
Cataract surgery: Cataract extraction may improve visual function when lens opacity becomes clinically significant.
Surgical management: Surgery remains the main treatment for localized PPGL and somatostatinoma lesions, but repeated interventions are often required because tumors are multifocal and recurrent.

Belzutifan: Selective HIF-2alpha inhibition with belzutifan has produced a rapid and sustained response in an EPAS1-mosaic patient, improving tumor burden, hypertension, headaches, and long-standing polycythemia.
Cognitive behavioral therapy: CBT, including exposure-based, guided self-help, face-to-face individual, and group delivery formats, is an evidence-supported psychotherapy for panic disorder.

SSRI and SNRI pharmacotherapy: Serotonergic antidepressant pharmacotherapy for panic disorder includes SSRIs and SNRIs, with paroxetine and venlafaxine represented here from a panic-disorder clinical trial.
Benzodiazepine pharmacotherapy: Benzodiazepine pharmacotherapy with clonazepam or alprazolam is represented as an evidence-supported medication option for panic disorder.
Symptomatic pharmacotherapy: PKAN is treated symptomatically with medications for dystonia and related motor features.
Iron chelation therapy: Iron chelation is used to target the iron overload component of PKAN.
Deep brain stimulation: Deep brain stimulation can improve dystonia but does not alter the disease course.
Pantothenate replacement therapy: Phosphopantothenate replacement is a rational pathway-bypass strategy under development for PKAN.
Metabolic supplement therapy: Commercial supplements have been explored to reduce iron accumulation and improve cellular phenotypes in residual PANK2-expressing models.
Thyroidectomy: Surgical resection is the mainstay of treatment for localized papillary thyroid carcinoma.
Radioiodine Therapy: Radioactive iodine is used selectively after surgery for intermediate-risk, high-risk, or metastatic differentiated thyroid cancer.

Mutation-Directed Targeted Therapy: Advanced papillary thyroid carcinoma with actionable driver alterations may be treated with genotype-matched targeted therapy, including RET inhibitors for RET fusion-positive disease and BRAF-directed regimens in BRAF V600E- driven refractory tumors.

Lenvatinib or Sorafenib: Multi-kinase inhibitors are standard systemic options for progressive radioiodine-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer when surgery and radioiodine are no longer sufficient.
Supportive care: Symptom-directed multidisciplinary support is the mainstay of management because there is no disease-specific curative therapy.
Speech therapy: Speech and language therapy is appropriate for the prominent language deficit in the syndrome.
Physical therapy: Physical therapy can address hypotonia and motor delay.
Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling is recommended for families because pathogenic CHD5 variants are typically de novo but may also be inherited.

Anticonvulsant agent therapy: Antiseizure medications remain the baseline symptomatic treatment for the epilepsy component of FAME, although they often do not adequately control cortical tremor or myoclonus.

payload.disease: Pentanucleotide Repeat Familial Adult Myoclonus Epilepsy

text: Perampanel therapy: Low-dose perampanel is the best-supported targeted symptomatic therapy for cortical myoclonus in FAME and is associated with improved myoclonus scores together with reduced sensorimotor hyperexcitability.

payload.disease: Pentanucleotide Repeat Familial Adult Myoclonus Epilepsy

text: Deep brain stimulation (experimental): Thalamic ventral intermediate nucleus deep brain stimulation is being explored as an adjunctive intervention for refractory familial cortical myoclonus with epilepsy.

payload.disease: Pentanucleotide Repeat Familial Adult Myoclonus Epilepsy

text: Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling is important because the disorder is autosomal dominant, can show anticipation, and is commonly misdiagnosed before molecular confirmation.

payload.disease: Pentanucleotide Repeat Familial Adult Myoclonus Epilepsy

text: CHOP-based Multiagent Chemotherapy: Anthracycline-based combination chemotherapy remains the historic frontline backbone for many PTCL cases, although durability is limited.
Brentuximab Vedotin Plus CHP: Frontline regimen for CD30-expressing PTCL, especially systemic ALCL and selected PTCL-NOS and TFH cases with CD30 expression.
Pralatrexate: Antifolate therapy used in relapsed or refractory PTCL after prior systemic treatment.
Romidepsin: Histone deacetylase inhibitor with single-agent activity in relapsed PTCL.
Autologous Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation: Consolidative transplant strategy considered in fit patients with responsive disease, particularly in first remission or chemosensitive relapse.
Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation: Allogeneic transplantation remains an option for selected relapsed PTCL cases, particularly when graft-versus-lymphoma activity is sought.
Gross Total Resection: Gross total resection is preferred for surgically accessible tumors and remains the core first-line treatment for most cerebellar PA.

Carboplatin/Vincristine Chemotherapy: Carboplatin/vincristine remains a standard conventional regimen for residual, progressive, or unresectable pediatric low-grade glioma when surgery alone is insufficient and radiotherapy is undesirable.

Selumetinib: MEK inhibition with selumetinib is active in recurrent or progressive MAPK-driven low-grade glioma, including pilocytic astrocytoma and optic-pathway/hypothalamic subsets where visual preservation matters.

Tovorafenib: Tovorafenib is an FDA-approved type II RAF inhibitor for relapsed or refractory pediatric low-grade glioma with BRAF fusion, rearrangement, or V600E mutation, making it directly relevant to recurrent BRAF-altered PA.
Maximal Safe Surgical Resection: Initial management centers on maximal safe surgical excision for diagnosis, decompression, and cytoreduction before adjuvant therapy.
Craniospinal Irradiation: Craniospinal irradiation remains a core component of treatment for many patients, with dose adapted to risk group and age.
Multi-agent Chemotherapy: Chemotherapy is routinely combined with surgery and radiation, and in infants may be used before irradiation to delay or modify radiotherapy exposure.
Complete Tumor Resection: Complete surgical excision is a central therapeutic goal across PPB and is especially important for cystic type I disease to prevent progression.
Multi-Agent Chemotherapy: Multi-agent chemotherapy is a major component of PPB treatment, with IVADo favored for type II and type III disease in the registry era.
Radiation Therapy: Radiotherapy is used selectively as part of multimodality therapy for residual, unresectable, or advanced disease rather than as routine treatment for all PPB.

COX-2 Inhibitor (Etoricoxib): Selective COX-2 inhibitor etoricoxib (60 mg daily) reduces PGE2 synthesis and is the most studied targeted treatment for PHO. Shown to improve pachydermia, joint swelling, digital clubbing, and hyperhidrosis.
NSAIDs: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs provide symptomatic relief of arthralgia and were the mainstay of treatment before discovery of the underlying genetic cause.
Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling is recommended for affected families given the autosomal recessive and dominant inheritance patterns and the availability of molecular diagnosis.
Radical Prostatectomy: Surgical resection is a standard definitive option for higher-risk localized disease.
Radiation Therapy: External beam or related radiation approaches are standard definitive therapy for localized disease.
Androgen Deprivation Therapy: Medical castration with gonadotropin-releasing hormone pathway suppression is the backbone of systemic therapy for metastatic hormone-sensitive disease.
Abiraterone Acetate: CYP17-mediated androgen synthesis inhibition improves survival in metastatic castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma and is also used earlier in metastatic disease.
Enzalutamide: Second-generation androgen receptor inhibition is effective across nonmetastatic and metastatic castration-resistant settings.
Baricitinib therapy: JAK1/2 inhibition with baricitinib can suppress interferon-driven disease activity in PRAAS, but dose reductions may precipitate rebound flares.
Anifrolumab therapy: IFNAR1 blockade with anifrolumab is an emerging treatment option for type I interferonopathies and has early supportive evidence in CANDLE/PRAAS.
Triheptanoin supplementation: Triheptanoin is used as an anaplerotic odd-chain triglyceride therapy, with mixed overall results but apparent benefit in some type C patients.
Dietary long-chain triglyceride restriction: Dietary fat modification may accompany triheptanoin treatment in type C disease.

Multi-Agent Chemotherapy: Intensive multi-agent chemotherapy remains part of front-line treatment for most newly diagnosed patients, usually combined with surgery and local radiotherapy according to age, site, and resectability.
Gross Total Resection: Maximal safe resection is pursued whenever anatomically feasible because gross total resection is one of the strongest favorable prognostic factors in extracranial disease.
Radiation Therapy: Radiotherapy is used for local control, especially after incomplete resection or in high-risk disease, and contributes to outcome in multimodality therapy.

EZH2 Inhibitor Therapy: EZH2-targeted therapy is an investigational mechanism-based strategy for relapsed or refractory SMARCB1/SMARCA4-deficient disease. Preclinical data support strong EZH2 dependence, and early pediatric trials have tested tazemetostat in molecularly selected tumors.
Ruxolitinib therapy: JAK1/2 inhibition can improve interferon-driven inflammation and clinical status in SAVI, especially when started early, but disease control may remain incomplete.

payload.disease: STING-Associated Vasculopathy with Onset in Infancy

text: Baricitinib therapy: Baricitinib can reduce fever, skin disease, and respiratory symptoms in SAVI, but residual inflammation and progressive lung disease may persist.

payload.disease: STING-Associated Vasculopathy with Onset in Infancy

text: Supportive Care: Current management is supportive and palliative, with symptom-directed care for seizures, feeding, respiratory complications, and other neurologic disability.

Gene Therapy: Investigational AAV-mediated HEXB replacement, or coordinated HEXA/HEXB delivery, has shown strong preclinical rescue of enzyme activity, storage pathology, gliosis, survival, and motor function in Sandhoff mouse models.
4-Phenylbutyric Acid: 4-PBA is a preclinical pharmacologic strategy aimed at ER-stress-associated neuronal injury in Sandhoff disease.

Investigational gene therapy: Subtype-specific gene restoration strategies are in development to restore the missing lysosomal enzyme step and lower heparan sulfate storage. The strongest abstract-level mechanistic support in this curation comes from SGSH-directed type A models.
Substrate reduction therapy (genistein): Genistein has preclinical activity as a substrate reduction strategy in MPS IIIB, lowering storage burden, neuroinflammation, and behavioral abnormalities.
Supportive symptomatic care: Management remains largely supportive, with medications and specialist care directed toward behavioral symptoms, sleep disturbance, seizures, and other complications.

Supportive multidisciplinary care: Current treatment is supportive and focuses on seizure control, nutritional and developmental support, and surveillance for renal, respiratory, and oncologic complications.
Extracorporeal Photopheresis: Blood-directed extracorporeal photopheresis is a standard component of multimodality treatment for Sezary syndrome and can improve cutaneous and blood disease.
Bexarotene: Oral rexinoid therapy remains an established systemic option for advanced or refractory cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, including Sezary syndrome.
Mogamulizumab: CCR4-directed monoclonal antibody therapy has particular activity in blood-involved cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and is highly relevant to Sezary syndrome.
Pembrolizumab: PD-1 blockade has meaningful activity in advanced relapsed or refractory MF/SS and provides a rational option when systemic immune-targeted therapy is needed.
Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation: Allogeneic transplantation is the main potentially curative option for selected fit patients with advanced or multiply relapsed Sezary syndrome.

Quinidine/Hydroquinidine Therapy: Hydroquinidine is the best-supported pharmacologic therapy for SQTS. It prolongs the QT interval and ventricular refractory period and can suppress inducible or recurrent ventricular arrhythmias.

Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator: ICD implantation is used for secondary prevention or in high-risk patients, especially after aborted cardiac arrest or when malignant ventricular arrhythmia risk remains high.

Dietary cholesterol supplementation: Dietary cholesterol supplementation can raise plasma cholesterol and lower circulating 7DHC over time, although it does not fully correct all disease manifestations.
Simvastatin: Adjunctive simvastatin can improve the serum dehydrocholesterol-to-total sterol ratio and irritability in mild to classic SLOS with residual DHCR7 activity.
Cholic acid: Pilot bile-acid replacement with cholic acid increased plasma cholesterol in a small SLOS cohort, but current evidence is biochemical and preliminary.
Antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy: Antithrombotic pharmacotherapy is recommended to reduce recurrent ischemic cerebrovascular complications.
Combination vasculoprotective pharmacotherapy: Combination therapy adding alprostadil and captopril to standard antithrombotic treatment may reduce recurrent ischemic events.
Supervised rehabilitation program: Disease-specific rehabilitation is under active clinical evaluation for spastic ataxias.
Aerobic exercise-based rehabilitation: The IMPACT program is a structured exercise-based rehabilitation approach targeting gait, balance, and disease severity.
Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling is appropriate because the syndrome includes both chromosomal dosage-gain and autosomal recessive FIBP-related subtypes.

Multidisciplinary long-term follow-up: Multidisciplinary follow-up is relevant for TROFAS because later reports have broadened the subtype phenotype to include ophthalmologic, airway, skeletal, and cardiac manifestations.
Mexiletine therapy: Sodium-channel blocking therapy with mexiletine is commonly used for symptomatic reduction of myotonia.
Carbamazepine therapy: Carbamazepine is also used for symptomatic control of myotonia in some patients.
Physical therapy: Stretching, conditioning, and practical mobility strategies help patients manage stiffness and weakness.
Complete smoking cessation: Abstinence from tobacco and cannabis products remains the central disease- modifying intervention.

Intravenous iloprost-based conservative therapy: Intravenous iloprost has supportive clinical evidence as part of conservative treatment for TAO-related critical limb ischemia with rest pain and ulcer or necrotic lesions.

Intramuscular Stempeucel: Allogeneic bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stromal cell therapy has been studied for no-option critical limb ischemia due to TAO, with signals for reduced rest pain, ulcer healing, and collateral formation.
Bosentan: Endothelin-receptor antagonism has pilot-study evidence for improved ulcer, pain, distal flow, and endothelial function outcomes in refractory disease.
Thymectomy: Complete surgical resection is the central treatment for localized thymoma and can improve coexisting myasthenia gravis.
Postoperative radiotherapy: Adjuvant radiotherapy is used for invasive thymoma, incomplete resection, or aggressive histologic subtypes.
Platinum-anthracycline chemotherapy: Cisplatin-based multi-agent chemotherapy is used for unresectable, metastatic, or recurrent thymoma.
Systemic immunosuppression: Immunosuppressive therapy is often needed when thymoma-associated autoimmunity persists or emerges after thymectomy.
Beta-Blocker Therapy: Beta-blockers are standard first-line antiarrhythmic therapy in Timothy syndrome to blunt adrenergic triggering of malignant ventricular arrhythmias.

Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator (ICD): ICD placement is commonly used in high-risk patients because severe ventricular arrhythmias and aborted cardiac arrest remain common despite pharmacotherapy.

Pyridostigmine (Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitor): Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor trial showed increased CMAP amplitude but no functional improvement and caused copious secretions in the reported patient.
3,4-Diaminopyridine: Potassium channel blocker that enhances presynaptic calcium entry. Showed some improvement in CMAP amplitude and minimal clinical benefit.
Supportive Care: Ventilatory support, gastrostomy feeding, and seizure management form the mainstay of treatment in the severe congenital form.
Antiseizure Medication: Seizure management with antiepileptic drugs is a primary treatment approach, though response may vary depending on seizure type and underlying mechanism.

payload.disease: UNC13A-Related NDD with Seizures and Movement Disorder

text: Supportive Care and Rehabilitation: Speech therapy, physical therapy, and occupational therapy to address developmental delays and movement abnormalities.

payload.disease: UNC13A-Related NDD with Seizures and Movement Disorder

text: Supportive multidisciplinary care: No approved disease-modifying therapy is established for VCP-MSP myopathy, so care remains supportive and relies on standardized multidisciplinary management.
Physical therapy: Physical therapy is used as supportive rehabilitation to preserve mobility, function, and participation in progressive VCP myopathy.
Noninvasive ventilation: Ventilatory support is used in patients with respiratory involvement, especially when orthopnea or nocturnal insufficiency develops.
Guideline-directed heart failure pharmacotherapy: Management of VCP-associated cardiomyopathy currently extrapolates from standard heart-failure practice using neurohormonal blockade.
Genetic counseling: Genetic counseling and cascade testing are important because VCP-MSP is an autosomal dominant familial disorder with implications for at-risk relatives.

Congenital heart defect surgical palliation or repair: Management commonly requires staged univentricular palliation or selected biventricular repair according to the specific cardiac anatomy within the heterotaxy spectrum.

payload.treatment_name: Congenital heart defect surgical palliation or repair

text: Vaccination for asplenia or hyposplenism: When heterotaxy includes asplenia or splenic hypofunction, vaccination against encapsulated bacteria is a core infection-prevention strategy.
Antibiotic prophylaxis for asplenia or hyposplenism: Antibiotic prophylaxis is part of infection-prevention care for heterotaxy patients who have absent or functionally impaired spleens.

payload.treatment_name: Antibiotic prophylaxis for asplenia or hyposplenism

text: Watchful Waiting: Observation without immediate therapy is the standard disease-management strategy for asymptomatic WM without critically elevated IgM or compromised hematopoietic function.
Bendamustine-Rituximab: Fixed-duration bendamustine-rituximab chemoimmunotherapy remains a central first-line option for symptomatic treatment-naive WM.
Zanubrutinib: Zanubrutinib is a covalent BTK inhibitor option for symptomatic WM and shows lower toxicity and deeper remissions than ibrutinib in the cited randomized trial update.
Plasmapheresis: Plasmapheresis is used for rapid paraprotein reduction in hyperviscosity and related neurologic complications while systemic therapy addresses the underlying clone.

Sebelipase alfa enzyme replacement therapy: Recombinant human lysosomal acid lipase replacement restores proximal enzyme function and substantially improves survival, growth, liver parameters, and other major disease manifestations when started early.

Nutritional management with dietary lipid reduction: Nutritional management is used alongside enzyme replacement therapy to reduce dietary lipid substrate burden, improve gastrointestinal tolerance, and support growth in infants with severe intestinal disease.

payload.treatment_name: Nutritional management with dietary lipid reduction

text: Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation: Early allogeneic HSCT can halt progression of active cerebral disease in appropriately selected patients.

Hematopoietic stem cell gene therapy: Gene-modified autologous hematopoietic stem cells are an emerging strategy for cerebral ALD when a matched donor is not available or to improve treatment options.

Avoidance of Hyperthermia: Environmental modifications to prevent overheating, including cooling vests, air conditioning, avoidance of prolonged sun exposure, and monitoring during physical activity or febrile illness. This is critical in infancy when hyperthermia risk is greatest.

Dental Prosthetics and Implants: Early prosthetic dental rehabilitation with removable dentures in childhood, transitioning to dental implants in adolescence/adulthood. Addresses both functional (mastication, speech) and aesthetic concerns.

Prenatal EDA1 Replacement Therapy: Intra-amniotic administration of recombinant EDA1 replacement protein (Fc-EDA) during the second trimester can permanently correct sweat gland development. In treated infants, normal sweating ability was restored and persisted for at least six years. Postnatal administration was ineffective, indicating a narrow developmental window.

Genetic Counseling: X-linked recessive inheritance counseling for families. Carrier females have a 50% chance of affected sons. Carrier detection and prenatal/preimplantation genetic testing are available. Female carriers may exhibit mild features.
Cleft Lip/Palate Surgical Repair: Surgical correction of cleft lip and palate is the primary treatment intervention, typically performed in infancy with staged procedures.
Syndactyly Surgical Repair: Surgical correction of cutaneous syndactyly of fingers and/or toes. Syndactyly repair was documented in a molecularly confirmed CLPED1 patient.
Dental Implantation: Dental implantation and prosthetic replacement for missing teeth due to hypodontia from ectodermal dysplasia.

Genetic Counseling: Genetic counseling for families with affected children, given the autosomal recessive inheritance pattern, carrier detection options, and the known association of heterozygosity with non-syndromic CL/P risk.
Iron chelation therapy: Iron chelation is used to reduce systemic and neurologic iron-related toxicity.
Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation: Early hematopoietic stem cell transplantation is the key disease-modifying intervention for cerebral ALD.
Surgical treatment of symptomatic venous malformations: Selected patients with disabling venous symptoms may benefit from operative treatment of varicose veins and venous malformations.

payload.treatment_name: Surgical treatment of symptomatic venous malformations

text: PI3K/mTOR inhibitor pharmacotherapy: PI3K/mTOR-pathway inhibition can reduce experimental vascular malformation burden in PIK3CA-driven disease.
Acute hemodynamic support: Acute attacks are managed with cautious intravenous fluid resuscitation and hemodynamic support.
Theophylline prophylaxis: Oral theophylline has been used as prophylaxis against recurrent idiopathic systemic capillary leak attacks.
Aspirin: Aspirin can be effective particularly in secondary erythromelalgia related to myeloproliferative disease.
Multimodal pain management: Management is individualized and focuses on pain reduction, flare prevention, and avoidance of unsafe cooling behaviors.
Symptomatic pharmacotherapy: In the absence of a disease-modifying therapy, treatment is primarily symptomatic and targeted to the dominant movement disorder.
Arginine-restricted diet: Dietary arginine restriction is used to improve metabolic control and lower plasma ornithine burden.

Pyridoxine supplementation: Pyridoxine is used as adjunctive therapy in an attempt to improve metabolic control in some patients, although responsiveness is mutation-dependent and not universal. Related supplementation regimens may also include lysine, proline, or creatine in combination with arginine restriction.
Acetazolamide: Acetazolamide is the best-studied medical therapy and improves visual outcomes when combined with a weight-reduction program in patients with mild visual loss.
Weight-reduction intervention: Weight reduction is a core disease-directed intervention in overweight patients with IIH.
